2.3. Data Inventory and Assumptions. 2.3.1. Product Chemical Composition. The excretion rate and chemical composition of liquid manure in the ref was based on average data from the Dutch fattening pig sector (Table 1). The excretion rates and composition of urine and feces were based on an eight month measurement cycle, consisting of two growing periods, of an experimental setup of the Kempfarm
Table 3. Contribution of Substances to the Impact Categories for the Scenarios and Each Life Cycle Stage (LCS) Expressed per FU
"Totals include remaining substances and are rounded; therefore, they do not always correspond to the sum of the columns and rows. Seg =
segregation; st = storage; app = application; and fert = fertilizer.

12 system. Because the compositions of liquid manure, urine, and feces were taken from different literature sources and equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter had to enter the systems in the ref and scenarios for comparability reasons (FU), we scaled the compositions of urine and feces to those of liquid manure in the ref. This is illustrated in the Supporting Information for the total N composition. For the ref and scenarios, we calculated a mass balance to determine the compositions of the liquid manure and urine and feces in every life cycle stage (Table 1 and Table S1, Supporting Information).

2.3.2. Process Emissions. During storage, segregation, and field application, NH3, N2O, N, NO and CH4 emit as well as odor and nitrate (NO3-) leaches (Table 2). Emission factors for in-house storage and segregation were based on Dutch national data and ref 12, respectively. Emission factors for the Kempfarm system were measured over eight months (two growing periods) in a small-scale setup at a commercial farm. Ammonia concentrations were measured continuously, whereas greenhouse gases were measured four times (during 24 h for each sample) within the experiment. Emission factors for 12 N2O from storage of solid feces were presumed the same as those for solid manure storage (i.e., aerobic) and emission factors for storage of liquid feces were presumed the same as those for liquid manure storage (i.e., anaerobic). 30 CH4 emission factors were based on refs 2, 33, and 43. Methane emissions during storage of urine and feces were scaled relative to the ratio of emission from liquid manure in the ref (a factor of 42 times lower for solid feces and 12 times lower for urine)

based on a laboratory experiment. 43 In this study, liquid manure and separated solid and liquid fractions were stored for two months at 14 °C. Methane emissions were measured 20 times during the study.

On the basis of ref 2, we assumed emissions from application of urine to be similar to those of dewatered liquid fraction (mineral concentrate), whereas emissions from application of feces were assumed similar to those of the solid fraction remaining after liquid manure separation (Table 2). We included indirect N2O emissions from NH3 and NOx emission based on ref 42.

NO3 leaches from N application to the soil and can be calculated as a leached fraction of the soil N-surplus, that is, the difference between N-input into the soil and N-uptake by crops. 40,44 N-input into the soil and N-uptake by the crops depend, among others, on the chemical composition of fertilizer products (i.e., Nmin and Norg) and related gaseous emissions during application (NH3 and N2O). We included these factors by adjusting the NO3 leaching factor of each fertilizer product for their respective composition and gaseous emissions (Tables 1 and 2). Furthermore, we used a leaching fraction of 0.43 and a 95% confidence interval of 0.36-0.51. 45 This value represents a weighted average of leaching fractions on arable land with sandy (leaching fraction of 0.56) and clay (leaching fraction of 0.34) soils in The Netherlands.
2.3.3. Background Emissions. Background emissions are emissions not directly occurring from the management system but from supporting systems, that is, electricity production,

Figure 2. Results of the uncertainty analysis for the ref and scenarios with segregation after 1000 Monte Carlo simulations. Bars represent mean values; error bars represent the 95% confidence intervals. Different letters above bars indicate significant differences.
transportation, mineral fertilizer production, and capital goods production. All environmental impact data related to back- ground systems were taken from the Ecoinvent database. 19

2.4. Uncertainty Analysis. Uncertainty analysis was done to achieve insight in how variation in emission factors, that is, their 95% confidence intervals (95% CI), affected final results and the comparison of scenarios. We used Monte Carlo simulation (1000 runs) for the separate scenarios and for comparisons of scenarios to assess the probability of higher and lower outcomes of the respective comparison. Differences were assumed significant when outcomes were higher/lower in >97.5% of the comparisons (i.e., two-sided test at P < 0.05).

The following emission factors and related impact categories were included in the uncertainty analysis: N2O, NH3, CH4, and NO (CC, TA, PMF, and ME). For assessed emission factors, we defined a probability density function (PDF) and the 95% CI. PDFs and CIs were based on literature data (Table 2). Overall, PDFs were assumed as normal distributions (i.e., Gaussian), whereas for N2O emission we applied a log-normal distribution. 46

Ideally, within Monte Carlo simulation, a correlation matrix for dependent factors is established (e.g., when NH3 emission from application is low, N2O emission may be higher). However, due to the use of different literature sources to determine the 95% CI, we could not establish such correlations and assumed independence of factors.46,47 Where possible, however, dependency was included via calculation. NO and N2 emission from storage were calculated according to their ratio related to N2O emission and therefore are interrelated (Table 2). Similarly, NO3 leaching was calculated depending on gaseous emissions (N2O and NH3) (Section 2.3.2). Other factors affecting NO leaching, such as uncertainty in N- uptake by crops, were excluded. Overall, the dependent factors

will change according to the randomly selected values of the emission factors during Monte Carlo simulation. The assumed independency between emission factors however will most likely lead to an overestimated uncertainty in the final results.
20: 2.1. LCA Approach and Impact Assessment. We used LCA to compare the environmental impact of segregating pig urine and feces with the impact of conventional manure management, that is, liquid manure storage, transport, and field application. Environmental consequences from changing manure management were included in the system boundary (i.e., consequential LCA). 16 This implied that, for electricity supply and mineral fertilizer production, we used so-called marginal suppliers. Marginal suppliers were based on ref 2. The geographical scope of the study was Western Europe, where The Netherlands was considered for marginal suppliers, compositions of manure, urine and feces, and NO leaching.

We evaluated five environmental impact categories most relevant to manure management: climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), marine eutrophication (ME), particulate matter formation (PMF), and fossil fuel depletion (FFD). 2,9 For PMF, we included only NH3, NO and SO2 as precursors. Direct emission of PM 10 was excluded, because this was not affected by the different management systems. For 12 eutrophication, we excluded phosphorus (P) leaching as in all systems an equal amount of P entered and left the system. Field application was considered to be subject to equal management leading to unchanged leaching of P.1 To compare 2 the environmental impacts, we used the ReCiPe midpoint v.1.04
impact assessment method17 in SimaPro v.7.3 (PRé Con- sultants, The Netherlands).

2.2. Functional Unit and Manure Management Scenarios. 2.2.1. Functional Unit. The function of the considered system is to manage liquid manure or the segregated urine and feces. As the starting point is the excretion of urine and feces by the animal, we applied a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton mixed (i.e., liquid manure) or segregated fattening pig urine and feces originating from the same excreta to be able to compare scenarios. The same excreta ensured that equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter entered the system.

2.2.2. Manure Management Scenarios. The reference (ref) was based on conventional liquid manure management, including three months in-house storage under a slatted floor (60% of pen area), one month outside storage in a covered concrete tank, transport, and field application (Figure 1). The storage times represent yearly averages, as farmers are only allowed to apply manure from February 16 until the first of September in The Netherlands. 18 We assumed that manure was transported 31 km from the farm to the field by a lorry with a capacity of 32 tons. Subsequently, manure was injected into the soil of arable land with a conventional crop rotation, including potatoes, sugar beets, wheat, and onion, using a manure injector. 20 Manure application was assumed to substitute mineral N, P, and K-fertilizer by 62%, 100%, and 100%, respectively. 2,21

Scenarios with segregation of urine and feces included in- house segregation, four months outside storage of urine and feces separately (i.e., a yearly average), transport, and field application (Figure 1). Segregation was obtained by a V-shaped belt under a concrete slatted floor (see the Abstract graphic). Urine flows down the belt constantly, first to the middle of the belt and second to the end of the belt into a collection pit (i.e., by gravitational force). Feces were removed two times per day by rotating the belt. Outside storage of urine occurred in a covered concrete storage tank. Feces storage was presumed to be aerobic (as solid feces) or anaerobic (as liquid feces), depending on the use of bedding material. The use of bedding material will increase aeration in the storage of solid feces, leading to higher (de) nitrification and N2O emission. Currently, we do not know exactly at what feces composition (mainly dry matter content) the feces behave as "solid" or when feces behave as "liquid". Therefore, we assumed that solid and liquid feces have the same composition but can behave as

2.3. Data Inventory and Assumptions. 2.3.1. Product Chemical Composition. The excretion rate and chemical composition of liquid manure in the ref was based on average data from the Dutch fattening pig sector (Table 1). The excretion rates and composition of urine and feces were based on an eight month measurement cycle, consisting of two growing periods, of an experimental setup of the Kempfarm
Table 3. Contribution of Substances to the Impact Categories for the Scenarios and Each Life Cycle Stage (LCS) Expressed per FU
"Totals include remaining substances and are rounded; therefore, they do not always correspond to the sum of the columns and rows. Seg =
segregation; st = storage; app = application; and fert = fertilizer.

12 system. Because the compositions of liquid manure, urine, and feces were taken from different literature sources and equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter had to enter the systems in the ref and scenarios for comparability reasons (FU), we scaled the compositions of urine and feces to those of liquid manure in the ref. This is illustrated in the Supporting Information for the total N composition. For the ref and scenarios, we calculated a mass balance to determine the compositions of the liquid manure and urine and feces in every life cycle stage (Table 1 and Table S1, Supporting Information).

2.3.2. Process Emissions. During storage, segregation, and field application, NH3, N2O, N, NO and CH4 emit as well as odor and nitrate (NO3-) leaches (Table 2). Emission factors for in-house storage and segregation were based on Dutch national data and ref 12, respectively. Emission factors for the Kempfarm system were measured over eight months (two growing periods) in a small-scale setup at a commercial farm. Ammonia concentrations were measured continuously, whereas greenhouse gases were measured four times (during 24 h for each sample) within the experiment. Emission factors for 12 N2O from storage of solid feces were presumed the same as those for solid manure storage (i.e., aerobic) and emission factors for storage of liquid feces were presumed the same as those for liquid manure storage (i.e., anaerobic). 30 CH4 emission factors were based on refs 2, 33, and 43. Methane emissions during storage of urine and feces were scaled relative to the ratio of emission from liquid manure in the ref (a factor of 42 times lower for solid feces and 12 times lower for urine)

based on a laboratory experiment. 43 In this study, liquid manure and separated solid and liquid fractions were stored for two months at 14 °C. Methane emissions were measured 20 times during the study.

On the basis of ref 2, we assumed emissions from application of urine to be similar to those of dewatered liquid fraction (mineral concentrate), whereas emissions from application of feces were assumed similar to those of the solid fraction remaining after liquid manure separation (Table 2). We included indirect N2O emissions from NH3 and NOx emission based on ref 42.

NO3 leaches from N application to the soil and can be calculated as a leached fraction of the soil N-surplus, that is, the difference between N-input into the soil and N-uptake by crops. 40,44 N-input into the soil and N-uptake by the crops depend, among others, on the chemical composition of fertilizer products (i.e., Nmin and Norg) and related gaseous emissions during application (NH3 and N2O). We included these factors by adjusting the NO3 leaching factor of each fertilizer product for their respective composition and gaseous emissions (Tables 1 and 2). Furthermore, we used a leaching fraction of 0.43 and a 95% confidence interval of 0.36-0.51. 45 This value represents a weighted average of leaching fractions on arable land with sandy (leaching fraction of 0.56) and clay (leaching fraction of 0.34) soils in The Netherlands.
2.3.3. Background Emissions. Background emissions are emissions not directly occurring from the management system but from supporting systems, that is, electricity production,

Figure 2. Results of the uncertainty analysis for the ref and scenarios with segregation after 1000 Monte Carlo simulations. Bars represent mean values; error bars represent the 95% confidence intervals. Different letters above bars indicate significant differences.
transportation, mineral fertilizer production, and capital goods production. All environmental impact data related to back- ground systems were taken from the Ecoinvent database. 19

2.4. Uncertainty Analysis. Uncertainty analysis was done to achieve insight in how variation in emission factors, that is, their 95% confidence intervals (95% CI), affected final results and the comparison of scenarios. We used Monte Carlo simulation (1000 runs) for the separate scenarios and for comparisons of scenarios to assess the probability of higher and lower outcomes of the respective comparison. Differences were assumed significant when outcomes were higher/lower in >97.5% of the comparisons (i.e., two-sided test at P < 0.05).

The following emission factors and related impact categories were included in the uncertainty analysis: N2O, NH3, CH4, and NO (CC, TA, PMF, and ME). For assessed emission factors, we defined a probability density function (PDF) and the 95% CI. PDFs and CIs were based on literature data (Table 2). Overall, PDFs were assumed as normal distributions (i.e., Gaussian), whereas for N2O emission we applied a log-normal distribution. 46

Ideally, within Monte Carlo simulation, a correlation matrix for dependent factors is established (e.g., when NH3 emission from application is low, N2O emission may be higher). However, due to the use of different literature sources to determine the 95% CI, we could not establish such correlations and assumed independence of factors.46,47 Where possible, however, dependency was included via calculation. NO and N2 emission from storage were calculated according to their ratio related to N2O emission and therefore are interrelated (Table 2). Similarly, NO3 leaching was calculated depending on gaseous emissions (N2O and NH3) (Section 2.3.2). Other factors affecting NO leaching, such as uncertainty in N- uptake by crops, were excluded. Overall, the dependent factors

will change according to the randomly selected values of the emission factors during Monte Carlo simulation. The assumed independency between emission factors however will most likely lead to an overestimated uncertainty in the final results.
22: 2.1. LCA Approach and Impact Assessment. We used LCA to compare the environmental impact of segregating pig urine and feces with the impact of conventional manure management, that is, liquid manure storage, transport, and field application. Environmental consequences from changing manure management were included in the system boundary (i.e., consequential LCA). 16 This implied that, for electricity supply and mineral fertilizer production, we used so-called marginal suppliers. Marginal suppliers were based on ref 2. The geographical scope of the study was Western Europe, where The Netherlands was considered for marginal suppliers, compositions of manure, urine and feces, and NO leaching.

We evaluated five environmental impact categories most relevant to manure management: climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), marine eutrophication (ME), particulate matter formation (PMF), and fossil fuel depletion (FFD). 2,9 For PMF, we included only NH3, NO and SO2 as precursors. Direct emission of PM 10 was excluded, because this was not affected by the different management systems. For 12 eutrophication, we excluded phosphorus (P) leaching as in all systems an equal amount of P entered and left the system. Field application was considered to be subject to equal management leading to unchanged leaching of P.1 To compare 2 the environmental impacts, we used the ReCiPe midpoint v.1.04
impact assessment method17 in SimaPro v.7.3 (PRé Con- sultants, The Netherlands).

2.2. Functional Unit and Manure Management Scenarios. 2.2.1. Functional Unit. The function of the considered system is to manage liquid manure or the segregated urine and feces. As the starting point is the excretion of urine and feces by the animal, we applied a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton mixed (i.e., liquid manure) or segregated fattening pig urine and feces originating from the same excreta to be able to compare scenarios. The same excreta ensured that equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter entered the system.

2.2.2. Manure Management Scenarios. The reference (ref) was based on conventional liquid manure management, including three months in-house storage under a slatted floor (60% of pen area), one month outside storage in a covered concrete tank, transport, and field application (Figure 1). The storage times represent yearly averages, as farmers are only allowed to apply manure from February 16 until the first of September in The Netherlands. 18 We assumed that manure was transported 31 km from the farm to the field by a lorry with a capacity of 32 tons. Subsequently, manure was injected into the soil of arable land with a conventional crop rotation, including potatoes, sugar beets, wheat, and onion, using a manure injector. 20 Manure application was assumed to substitute mineral N, P, and K-fertilizer by 62%, 100%, and 100%, respectively. 2,21

Scenarios with segregation of urine and feces included in- house segregation, four months outside storage of urine and feces separately (i.e., a yearly average), transport, and field application (Figure 1). Segregation was obtained by a V-shaped belt under a concrete slatted floor (see the Abstract graphic). Urine flows down the belt constantly, first to the middle of the belt and second to the end of the belt into a collection pit (i.e., by gravitational force). Feces were removed two times per day by rotating the belt. Outside storage of urine occurred in a covered concrete storage tank. Feces storage was presumed to be aerobic (as solid feces) or anaerobic (as liquid feces), depending on the use of bedding material. The use of bedding material will increase aeration in the storage of solid feces, leading to higher (de) nitrification and N2O emission. Currently, we do not know exactly at what feces composition (mainly dry matter content) the feces behave as "solid" or when feces behave as "liquid". Therefore, we assumed that solid and liquid feces have the same composition but can behave as

2.3. Data Inventory and Assumptions. 2.3.1. Product Chemical Composition. The excretion rate and chemical composition of liquid manure in the ref was based on average data from the Dutch fattening pig sector (Table 1). The excretion rates and composition of urine and feces were based on an eight month measurement cycle, consisting of two growing periods, of an experimental setup of the Kempfarm
Table 3. Contribution of Substances to the Impact Categories for the Scenarios and Each Life Cycle Stage (LCS) Expressed per FU
"Totals include remaining substances and are rounded; therefore, they do not always correspond to the sum of the columns and rows. Seg =
segregation; st = storage; app = application; and fert = fertilizer.

12 system. Because the compositions of liquid manure, urine, and feces were taken from different literature sources and equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter had to enter the systems in the ref and scenarios for comparability reasons (FU), we scaled the compositions of urine and feces to those of liquid manure in the ref. This is illustrated in the Supporting Information for the total N composition. For the ref and scenarios, we calculated a mass balance to determine the compositions of the liquid manure and urine and feces in every life cycle stage (Table 1 and Table S1, Supporting Information).

2.3.2. Process Emissions. During storage, segregation, and field application, NH3, N2O, N, NO and CH4 emit as well as odor and nitrate (NO3-) leaches (Table 2). Emission factors for in-house storage and segregation were based on Dutch national data and ref 12, respectively. Emission factors for the Kempfarm system were measured over eight months (two growing periods) in a small-scale setup at a commercial farm. Ammonia concentrations were measured continuously, whereas greenhouse gases were measured four times (during 24 h for each sample) within the experiment. Emission factors for 12 N2O from storage of solid feces were presumed the same as those for solid manure storage (i.e., aerobic) and emission factors for storage of liquid feces were presumed the same as those for liquid manure storage (i.e., anaerobic). 30 CH4 emission factors were based on refs 2, 33, and 43. Methane emissions during storage of urine and feces were scaled relative to the ratio of emission from liquid manure in the ref (a factor of 42 times lower for solid feces and 12 times lower for urine)

based on a laboratory experiment. 43 In this study, liquid manure and separated solid and liquid fractions were stored for two months at 14 °C. Methane emissions were measured 20 times during the study.

On the basis of ref 2, we assumed emissions from application of urine to be similar to those of dewatered liquid fraction (mineral concentrate), whereas emissions from application of feces were assumed similar to those of the solid fraction remaining after liquid manure separation (Table 2). We included indirect N2O emissions from NH3 and NOx emission based on ref 42.

NO3 leaches from N application to the soil and can be calculated as a leached fraction of the soil N-surplus, that is, the difference between N-input into the soil and N-uptake by crops. 40,44 N-input into the soil and N-uptake by the crops depend, among others, on the chemical composition of fertilizer products (i.e., Nmin and Norg) and related gaseous emissions during application (NH3 and N2O). We included these factors by adjusting the NO3 leaching factor of each fertilizer product for their respective composition and gaseous emissions (Tables 1 and 2). Furthermore, we used a leaching fraction of 0.43 and a 95% confidence interval of 0.36-0.51. 45 This value represents a weighted average of leaching fractions on arable land with sandy (leaching fraction of 0.56) and clay (leaching fraction of 0.34) soils in The Netherlands.
2.3.3. Background Emissions. Background emissions are emissions not directly occurring from the management system but from supporting systems, that is, electricity production,

Figure 2. Results of the uncertainty analysis for the ref and scenarios with segregation after 1000 Monte Carlo simulations. Bars represent mean values; error bars represent the 95% confidence intervals. Different letters above bars indicate significant differences.
transportation, mineral fertilizer production, and capital goods production. All environmental impact data related to back- ground systems were taken from the Ecoinvent database. 19

2.4. Uncertainty Analysis. Uncertainty analysis was done to achieve insight in how variation in emission factors, that is, their 95% confidence intervals (95% CI), affected final results and the comparison of scenarios. We used Monte Carlo simulation (1000 runs) for the separate scenarios and for comparisons of scenarios to assess the probability of higher and lower outcomes of the respective comparison. Differences were assumed significant when outcomes were higher/lower in >97.5% of the comparisons (i.e., two-sided test at P < 0.05).

The following emission factors and related impact categories were included in the uncertainty analysis: N2O, NH3, CH4, and NO (CC, TA, PMF, and ME). For assessed emission factors, we defined a probability density function (PDF) and the 95% CI. PDFs and CIs were based on literature data (Table 2). Overall, PDFs were assumed as normal distributions (i.e., Gaussian), whereas for N2O emission we applied a log-normal distribution. 46

Ideally, within Monte Carlo simulation, a correlation matrix for dependent factors is established (e.g., when NH3 emission from application is low, N2O emission may be higher). However, due to the use of different literature sources to determine the 95% CI, we could not establish such correlations and assumed independence of factors.46,47 Where possible, however, dependency was included via calculation. NO and N2 emission from storage were calculated according to their ratio related to N2O emission and therefore are interrelated (Table 2). Similarly, NO3 leaching was calculated depending on gaseous emissions (N2O and NH3) (Section 2.3.2). Other factors affecting NO leaching, such as uncertainty in N- uptake by crops, were excluded. Overall, the dependent factors

will change according to the randomly selected values of the emission factors during Monte Carlo simulation. The assumed independency between emission factors however will most likely lead to an overestimated uncertainty in the final results.
24: 2.1. LCA Approach and Impact Assessment. We used LCA to compare the environmental impact of segregating pig urine and feces with the impact of conventional manure management, that is, liquid manure storage, transport, and field application. Environmental consequences from changing manure management were included in the system boundary (i.e., consequential LCA). 16 This implied that, for electricity supply and mineral fertilizer production, we used so-called marginal suppliers. Marginal suppliers were based on ref 2. The geographical scope of the study was Western Europe, where The Netherlands was considered for marginal suppliers, compositions of manure, urine and feces, and NO leaching.

We evaluated five environmental impact categories most relevant to manure management: climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), marine eutrophication (ME), particulate matter formation (PMF), and fossil fuel depletion (FFD). 2,9 For PMF, we included only NH3, NO and SO2 as precursors. Direct emission of PM 10 was excluded, because this was not affected by the different management systems. For 12 eutrophication, we excluded phosphorus (P) leaching as in all systems an equal amount of P entered and left the system. Field application was considered to be subject to equal management leading to unchanged leaching of P.1 To compare 2 the environmental impacts, we used the ReCiPe midpoint v.1.04
impact assessment method17 in SimaPro v.7.3 (PRé Con- sultants, The Netherlands).

2.2. Functional Unit and Manure Management Scenarios. 2.2.1. Functional Unit. The function of the considered system is to manage liquid manure or the segregated urine and feces. As the starting point is the excretion of urine and feces by the animal, we applied a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton mixed (i.e., liquid manure) or segregated fattening pig urine and feces originating from the same excreta to be able to compare scenarios. The same excreta ensured that equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter entered the system.

2.2.2. Manure Management Scenarios. The reference (ref) was based on conventional liquid manure management, including three months in-house storage under a slatted floor (60% of pen area), one month outside storage in a covered concrete tank, transport, and field application (Figure 1). The storage times represent yearly averages, as farmers are only allowed to apply manure from February 16 until the first of September in The Netherlands. 18 We assumed that manure was transported 31 km from the farm to the field by a lorry with a capacity of 32 tons. Subsequently, manure was injected into the soil of arable land with a conventional crop rotation, including potatoes, sugar beets, wheat, and onion, using a manure injector. 20 Manure application was assumed to substitute mineral N, P, and K-fertilizer by 62%, 100%, and 100%, respectively. 2,21

Scenarios with segregation of urine and feces included in- house segregation, four months outside storage of urine and feces separately (i.e., a yearly average), transport, and field application (Figure 1). Segregation was obtained by a V-shaped belt under a concrete slatted floor (see the Abstract graphic). Urine flows down the belt constantly, first to the middle of the belt and second to the end of the belt into a collection pit (i.e., by gravitational force). Feces were removed two times per day by rotating the belt. Outside storage of urine occurred in a covered concrete storage tank. Feces storage was presumed to be aerobic (as solid feces) or anaerobic (as liquid feces), depending on the use of bedding material. The use of bedding material will increase aeration in the storage of solid feces, leading to higher (de) nitrification and N2O emission. Currently, we do not know exactly at what feces composition (mainly dry matter content) the feces behave as "solid" or when feces behave as "liquid". Therefore, we assumed that solid and liquid feces have the same composition but can behave as

2.3. Data Inventory and Assumptions. 2.3.1. Product Chemical Composition. The excretion rate and chemical composition of liquid manure in the ref was based on average data from the Dutch fattening pig sector (Table 1). The excretion rates and composition of urine and feces were based on an eight month measurement cycle, consisting of two growing periods, of an experimental setup of the Kempfarm
Table 3. Contribution of Substances to the Impact Categories for the Scenarios and Each Life Cycle Stage (LCS) Expressed per FU
"Totals include remaining substances and are rounded; therefore, they do not always correspond to the sum of the columns and rows. Seg =
segregation; st = storage; app = application; and fert = fertilizer.

12 system. Because the compositions of liquid manure, urine, and feces were taken from different literature sources and equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter had to enter the systems in the ref and scenarios for comparability reasons (FU), we scaled the compositions of urine and feces to those of liquid manure in the ref. This is illustrated in the Supporting Information for the total N composition. For the ref and scenarios, we calculated a mass balance to determine the compositions of the liquid manure and urine and feces in every life cycle stage (Table 1 and Table S1, Supporting Information).

2.3.2. Process Emissions. During storage, segregation, and field application, NH3, N2O, N, NO and CH4 emit as well as odor and nitrate (NO3-) leaches (Table 2). Emission factors for in-house storage and segregation were based on Dutch national data and ref 12, respectively. Emission factors for the Kempfarm system were measured over eight months (two growing periods) in a small-scale setup at a commercial farm. Ammonia concentrations were measured continuously, whereas greenhouse gases were measured four times (during 24 h for each sample) within the experiment. Emission factors for 12 N2O from storage of solid feces were presumed the same as those for solid manure storage (i.e., aerobic) and emission factors for storage of liquid feces were presumed the same as those for liquid manure storage (i.e., anaerobic). 30 CH4 emission factors were based on refs 2, 33, and 43. Methane emissions during storage of urine and feces were scaled relative to the ratio of emission from liquid manure in the ref (a factor of 42 times lower for solid feces and 12 times lower for urine)

based on a laboratory experiment. 43 In this study, liquid manure and separated solid and liquid fractions were stored for two months at 14 °C. Methane emissions were measured 20 times during the study.

On the basis of ref 2, we assumed emissions from application of urine to be similar to those of dewatered liquid fraction (mineral concentrate), whereas emissions from application of feces were assumed similar to those of the solid fraction remaining after liquid manure separation (Table 2). We included indirect N2O emissions from NH3 and NOx emission based on ref 42.

NO3 leaches from N application to the soil and can be calculated as a leached fraction of the soil N-surplus, that is, the difference between N-input into the soil and N-uptake by crops. 40,44 N-input into the soil and N-uptake by the crops depend, among others, on the chemical composition of fertilizer products (i.e., Nmin and Norg) and related gaseous emissions during application (NH3 and N2O). We included these factors by adjusting the NO3 leaching factor of each fertilizer product for their respective composition and gaseous emissions (Tables 1 and 2). Furthermore, we used a leaching fraction of 0.43 and a 95% confidence interval of 0.36-0.51. 45 This value represents a weighted average of leaching fractions on arable land with sandy (leaching fraction of 0.56) and clay (leaching fraction of 0.34) soils in The Netherlands.
2.3.3. Background Emissions. Background emissions are emissions not directly occurring from the management system but from supporting systems, that is, electricity production,

Figure 2. Results of the uncertainty analysis for the ref and scenarios with segregation after 1000 Monte Carlo simulations. Bars represent mean values; error bars represent the 95% confidence intervals. Different letters above bars indicate significant differences.
transportation, mineral fertilizer production, and capital goods production. All environmental impact data related to back- ground systems were taken from the Ecoinvent database. 19

2.4. Uncertainty Analysis. Uncertainty analysis was done to achieve insight in how variation in emission factors, that is, their 95% confidence intervals (95% CI), affected final results and the comparison of scenarios. We used Monte Carlo simulation (1000 runs) for the separate scenarios and for comparisons of scenarios to assess the probability of higher and lower outcomes of the respective comparison. Differences were assumed significant when outcomes were higher/lower in >97.5% of the comparisons (i.e., two-sided test at P < 0.05).

The following emission factors and related impact categories were included in the uncertainty analysis: N2O, NH3, CH4, and NO (CC, TA, PMF, and ME). For assessed emission factors, we defined a probability density function (PDF) and the 95% CI. PDFs and CIs were based on literature data (Table 2). Overall, PDFs were assumed as normal distributions (i.e., Gaussian), whereas for N2O emission we applied a log-normal distribution. 46

Ideally, within Monte Carlo simulation, a correlation matrix for dependent factors is established (e.g., when NH3 emission from application is low, N2O emission may be higher). However, due to the use of different literature sources to determine the 95% CI, we could not establish such correlations and assumed independence of factors.46,47 Where possible, however, dependency was included via calculation. NO and N2 emission from storage were calculated according to their ratio related to N2O emission and therefore are interrelated (Table 2). Similarly, NO3 leaching was calculated depending on gaseous emissions (N2O and NH3) (Section 2.3.2). Other factors affecting NO leaching, such as uncertainty in N- uptake by crops, were excluded. Overall, the dependent factors

will change according to the randomly selected values of the emission factors during Monte Carlo simulation. The assumed independency between emission factors however will most likely lead to an overestimated uncertainty in the final results.
26: 2.1. LCA Approach and Impact Assessment. We used LCA to compare the environmental impact of segregating pig urine and feces with the impact of conventional manure management, that is, liquid manure storage, transport, and field application. Environmental consequences from changing manure management were included in the system boundary (i.e., consequential LCA). 16 This implied that, for electricity supply and mineral fertilizer production, we used so-called marginal suppliers. Marginal suppliers were based on ref 2. The geographical scope of the study was Western Europe, where The Netherlands was considered for marginal suppliers, compositions of manure, urine and feces, and NO leaching.

We evaluated five environmental impact categories most relevant to manure management: climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), marine eutrophication (ME), particulate matter formation (PMF), and fossil fuel depletion (FFD). 2,9 For PMF, we included only NH3, NO and SO2 as precursors. Direct emission of PM 10 was excluded, because this was not affected by the different management systems. For 12 eutrophication, we excluded phosphorus (P) leaching as in all systems an equal amount of P entered and left the system. Field application was considered to be subject to equal management leading to unchanged leaching of P.1 To compare 2 the environmental impacts, we used the ReCiPe midpoint v.1.04
impact assessment method17 in SimaPro v.7.3 (PRé Con- sultants, The Netherlands).

2.2. Functional Unit and Manure Management Scenarios. 2.2.1. Functional Unit. The function of the considered system is to manage liquid manure or the segregated urine and feces. As the starting point is the excretion of urine and feces by the animal, we applied a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton mixed (i.e., liquid manure) or segregated fattening pig urine and feces originating from the same excreta to be able to compare scenarios. The same excreta ensured that equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter entered the system.

2.2.2. Manure Management Scenarios. The reference (ref) was based on conventional liquid manure management, including three months in-house storage under a slatted floor (60% of pen area), one month outside storage in a covered concrete tank, transport, and field application (Figure 1). The storage times represent yearly averages, as farmers are only allowed to apply manure from February 16 until the first of September in The Netherlands. 18 We assumed that manure was transported 31 km from the farm to the field by a lorry with a capacity of 32 tons. Subsequently, manure was injected into the soil of arable land with a conventional crop rotation, including potatoes, sugar beets, wheat, and onion, using a manure injector. 20 Manure application was assumed to substitute mineral N, P, and K-fertilizer by 62%, 100%, and 100%, respectively. 2,21

Scenarios with segregation of urine and feces included in- house segregation, four months outside storage of urine and feces separately (i.e., a yearly average), transport, and field application (Figure 1). Segregation was obtained by a V-shaped belt under a concrete slatted floor (see the Abstract graphic). Urine flows down the belt constantly, first to the middle of the belt and second to the end of the belt into a collection pit (i.e., by gravitational force). Feces were removed two times per day by rotating the belt. Outside storage of urine occurred in a covered concrete storage tank. Feces storage was presumed to be aerobic (as solid feces) or anaerobic (as liquid feces), depending on the use of bedding material. The use of bedding material will increase aeration in the storage of solid feces, leading to higher (de) nitrification and N2O emission. Currently, we do not know exactly at what feces composition (mainly dry matter content) the feces behave as "solid" or when feces behave as "liquid". Therefore, we assumed that solid and liquid feces have the same composition but can behave as

2.3. Data Inventory and Assumptions. 2.3.1. Product Chemical Composition. The excretion rate and chemical composition of liquid manure in the ref was based on average data from the Dutch fattening pig sector (Table 1). The excretion rates and composition of urine and feces were based on an eight month measurement cycle, consisting of two growing periods, of an experimental setup of the Kempfarm
Table 3. Contribution of Substances to the Impact Categories for the Scenarios and Each Life Cycle Stage (LCS) Expressed per FU
"Totals include remaining substances and are rounded; therefore, they do not always correspond to the sum of the columns and rows. Seg =
segregation; st = storage; app = application; and fert = fertilizer.

12 system. Because the compositions of liquid manure, urine, and feces were taken from different literature sources and equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter had to enter the systems in the ref and scenarios for comparability reasons (FU), we scaled the compositions of urine and feces to those of liquid manure in the ref. This is illustrated in the Supporting Information for the total N composition. For the ref and scenarios, we calculated a mass balance to determine the compositions of the liquid manure and urine and feces in every life cycle stage (Table 1 and Table S1, Supporting Information).

2.3.2. Process Emissions. During storage, segregation, and field application, NH3, N2O, N, NO and CH4 emit as well as odor and nitrate (NO3-) leaches (Table 2). Emission factors for in-house storage and segregation were based on Dutch national data and ref 12, respectively. Emission factors for the Kempfarm system were measured over eight months (two growing periods) in a small-scale setup at a commercial farm. Ammonia concentrations were measured continuously, whereas greenhouse gases were measured four times (during 24 h for each sample) within the experiment. Emission factors for 12 N2O from storage of solid feces were presumed the same as those for solid manure storage (i.e., aerobic) and emission factors for storage of liquid feces were presumed the same as those for liquid manure storage (i.e., anaerobic). 30 CH4 emission factors were based on refs 2, 33, and 43. Methane emissions during storage of urine and feces were scaled relative to the ratio of emission from liquid manure in the ref (a factor of 42 times lower for solid feces and 12 times lower for urine)

based on a laboratory experiment. 43 In this study, liquid manure and separated solid and liquid fractions were stored for two months at 14 °C. Methane emissions were measured 20 times during the study.

On the basis of ref 2, we assumed emissions from application of urine to be similar to those of dewatered liquid fraction (mineral concentrate), whereas emissions from application of feces were assumed similar to those of the solid fraction remaining after liquid manure separation (Table 2). We included indirect N2O emissions from NH3 and NOx emission based on ref 42.

NO3 leaches from N application to the soil and can be calculated as a leached fraction of the soil N-surplus, that is, the difference between N-input into the soil and N-uptake by crops. 40,44 N-input into the soil and N-uptake by the crops depend, among others, on the chemical composition of fertilizer products (i.e., Nmin and Norg) and related gaseous emissions during application (NH3 and N2O). We included these factors by adjusting the NO3 leaching factor of each fertilizer product for their respective composition and gaseous emissions (Tables 1 and 2). Furthermore, we used a leaching fraction of 0.43 and a 95% confidence interval of 0.36-0.51. 45 This value represents a weighted average of leaching fractions on arable land with sandy (leaching fraction of 0.56) and clay (leaching fraction of 0.34) soils in The Netherlands.
2.3.3. Background Emissions. Background emissions are emissions not directly occurring from the management system but from supporting systems, that is, electricity production,

Figure 2. Results of the uncertainty analysis for the ref and scenarios with segregation after 1000 Monte Carlo simulations. Bars represent mean values; error bars represent the 95% confidence intervals. Different letters above bars indicate significant differences.
transportation, mineral fertilizer production, and capital goods production. All environmental impact data related to back- ground systems were taken from the Ecoinvent database. 19

2.4. Uncertainty Analysis. Uncertainty analysis was done to achieve insight in how variation in emission factors, that is, their 95% confidence intervals (95% CI), affected final results and the comparison of scenarios. We used Monte Carlo simulation (1000 runs) for the separate scenarios and for comparisons of scenarios to assess the probability of higher and lower outcomes of the respective comparison. Differences were assumed significant when outcomes were higher/lower in >97.5% of the comparisons (i.e., two-sided test at P < 0.05).

The following emission factors and related impact categories were included in the uncertainty analysis: N2O, NH3, CH4, and NO (CC, TA, PMF, and ME). For assessed emission factors, we defined a probability density function (PDF) and the 95% CI. PDFs and CIs were based on literature data (Table 2). Overall, PDFs were assumed as normal distributions (i.e., Gaussian), whereas for N2O emission we applied a log-normal distribution. 46

Ideally, within Monte Carlo simulation, a correlation matrix for dependent factors is established (e.g., when NH3 emission from application is low, N2O emission may be higher). However, due to the use of different literature sources to determine the 95% CI, we could not establish such correlations and assumed independence of factors.46,47 Where possible, however, dependency was included via calculation. NO and N2 emission from storage were calculated according to their ratio related to N2O emission and therefore are interrelated (Table 2). Similarly, NO3 leaching was calculated depending on gaseous emissions (N2O and NH3) (Section 2.3.2). Other factors affecting NO leaching, such as uncertainty in N- uptake by crops, were excluded. Overall, the dependent factors

will change according to the randomly selected values of the emission factors during Monte Carlo simulation. The assumed independency between emission factors however will most likely lead to an overestimated uncertainty in the final results.
28: 2.1. LCA Approach and Impact Assessment. We used LCA to compare the environmental impact of segregating pig urine and feces with the impact of conventional manure management, that is, liquid manure storage, transport, and field application. Environmental consequences from changing manure management were included in the system boundary (i.e., consequential LCA). 16 This implied that, for electricity supply and mineral fertilizer production, we used so-called marginal suppliers. Marginal suppliers were based on ref 2. The geographical scope of the study was Western Europe, where The Netherlands was considered for marginal suppliers, compositions of manure, urine and feces, and NO leaching.

We evaluated five environmental impact categories most relevant to manure management: climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), marine eutrophication (ME), particulate matter formation (PMF), and fossil fuel depletion (FFD). 2,9 For PMF, we included only NH3, NO and SO2 as precursors. Direct emission of PM 10 was excluded, because this was not affected by the different management systems. For 12 eutrophication, we excluded phosphorus (P) leaching as in all systems an equal amount of P entered and left the system. Field application was considered to be subject to equal management leading to unchanged leaching of P.1 To compare 2 the environmental impacts, we used the ReCiPe midpoint v.1.04
impact assessment method17 in SimaPro v.7.3 (PRé Con- sultants, The Netherlands).

2.2. Functional Unit and Manure Management Scenarios. 2.2.1. Functional Unit. The function of the considered system is to manage liquid manure or the segregated urine and feces. As the starting point is the excretion of urine and feces by the animal, we applied a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton mixed (i.e., liquid manure) or segregated fattening pig urine and feces originating from the same excreta to be able to compare scenarios. The same excreta ensured that equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter entered the system.

2.2.2. Manure Management Scenarios. The reference (ref) was based on conventional liquid manure management, including three months in-house storage under a slatted floor (60% of pen area), one month outside storage in a covered concrete tank, transport, and field application (Figure 1). The storage times represent yearly averages, as farmers are only allowed to apply manure from February 16 until the first of September in The Netherlands. 18 We assumed that manure was transported 31 km from the farm to the field by a lorry with a capacity of 32 tons. Subsequently, manure was injected into the soil of arable land with a conventional crop rotation, including potatoes, sugar beets, wheat, and onion, using a manure injector. 20 Manure application was assumed to substitute mineral N, P, and K-fertilizer by 62%, 100%, and 100%, respectively. 2,21

Scenarios with segregation of urine and feces included in- house segregation, four months outside storage of urine and feces separately (i.e., a yearly average), transport, and field application (Figure 1). Segregation was obtained by a V-shaped belt under a concrete slatted floor (see the Abstract graphic). Urine flows down the belt constantly, first to the middle of the belt and second to the end of the belt into a collection pit (i.e., by gravitational force). Feces were removed two times per day by rotating the belt. Outside storage of urine occurred in a covered concrete storage tank. Feces storage was presumed to be aerobic (as solid feces) or anaerobic (as liquid feces), depending on the use of bedding material. The use of bedding material will increase aeration in the storage of solid feces, leading to higher (de) nitrification and N2O emission. Currently, we do not know exactly at what feces composition (mainly dry matter content) the feces behave as "solid" or when feces behave as "liquid". Therefore, we assumed that solid and liquid feces have the same composition but can behave as

2.3. Data Inventory and Assumptions. 2.3.1. Product Chemical Composition. The excretion rate and chemical composition of liquid manure in the ref was based on average data from the Dutch fattening pig sector (Table 1). The excretion rates and composition of urine and feces were based on an eight month measurement cycle, consisting of two growing periods, of an experimental setup of the Kempfarm
Table 3. Contribution of Substances to the Impact Categories for the Scenarios and Each Life Cycle Stage (LCS) Expressed per FU
"Totals include remaining substances and are rounded; therefore, they do not always correspond to the sum of the columns and rows. Seg =
segregation; st = storage; app = application; and fert = fertilizer.

12 system. Because the compositions of liquid manure, urine, and feces were taken from different literature sources and equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter had to enter the systems in the ref and scenarios for comparability reasons (FU), we scaled the compositions of urine and feces to those of liquid manure in the ref. This is illustrated in the Supporting Information for the total N composition. For the ref and scenarios, we calculated a mass balance to determine the compositions of the liquid manure and urine and feces in every life cycle stage (Table 1 and Table S1, Supporting Information).

2.3.2. Process Emissions. During storage, segregation, and field application, NH3, N2O, N, NO and CH4 emit as well as odor and nitrate (NO3-) leaches (Table 2). Emission factors for in-house storage and segregation were based on Dutch national data and ref 12, respectively. Emission factors for the Kempfarm system were measured over eight months (two growing periods) in a small-scale setup at a commercial farm. Ammonia concentrations were measured continuously, whereas greenhouse gases were measured four times (during 24 h for each sample) within the experiment. Emission factors for 12 N2O from storage of solid feces were presumed the same as those for solid manure storage (i.e., aerobic) and emission factors for storage of liquid feces were presumed the same as those for liquid manure storage (i.e., anaerobic). 30 CH4 emission factors were based on refs 2, 33, and 43. Methane emissions during storage of urine and feces were scaled relative to the ratio of emission from liquid manure in the ref (a factor of 42 times lower for solid feces and 12 times lower for urine)

based on a laboratory experiment. 43 In this study, liquid manure and separated solid and liquid fractions were stored for two months at 14 °C. Methane emissions were measured 20 times during the study.

On the basis of ref 2, we assumed emissions from application of urine to be similar to those of dewatered liquid fraction (mineral concentrate), whereas emissions from application of feces were assumed similar to those of the solid fraction remaining after liquid manure separation (Table 2). We included indirect N2O emissions from NH3 and NOx emission based on ref 42.

NO3 leaches from N application to the soil and can be calculated as a leached fraction of the soil N-surplus, that is, the difference between N-input into the soil and N-uptake by crops. 40,44 N-input into the soil and N-uptake by the crops depend, among others, on the chemical composition of fertilizer products (i.e., Nmin and Norg) and related gaseous emissions during application (NH3 and N2O). We included these factors by adjusting the NO3 leaching factor of each fertilizer product for their respective composition and gaseous emissions (Tables 1 and 2). Furthermore, we used a leaching fraction of 0.43 and a 95% confidence interval of 0.36-0.51. 45 This value represents a weighted average of leaching fractions on arable land with sandy (leaching fraction of 0.56) and clay (leaching fraction of 0.34) soils in The Netherlands.
2.3.3. Background Emissions. Background emissions are emissions not directly occurring from the management system but from supporting systems, that is, electricity production,

Figure 2. Results of the uncertainty analysis for the ref and scenarios with segregation after 1000 Monte Carlo simulations. Bars represent mean values; error bars represent the 95% confidence intervals. Different letters above bars indicate significant differences.
transportation, mineral fertilizer production, and capital goods production. All environmental impact data related to back- ground systems were taken from the Ecoinvent database. 19

2.4. Uncertainty Analysis. Uncertainty analysis was done to achieve insight in how variation in emission factors, that is, their 95% confidence intervals (95% CI), affected final results and the comparison of scenarios. We used Monte Carlo simulation (1000 runs) for the separate scenarios and for comparisons of scenarios to assess the probability of higher and lower outcomes of the respective comparison. Differences were assumed significant when outcomes were higher/lower in >97.5% of the comparisons (i.e., two-sided test at P < 0.05).

The following emission factors and related impact categories were included in the uncertainty analysis: N2O, NH3, CH4, and NO (CC, TA, PMF, and ME). For assessed emission factors, we defined a probability density function (PDF) and the 95% CI. PDFs and CIs were based on literature data (Table 2). Overall, PDFs were assumed as normal distributions (i.e., Gaussian), whereas for N2O emission we applied a log-normal distribution. 46

Ideally, within Monte Carlo simulation, a correlation matrix for dependent factors is established (e.g., when NH3 emission from application is low, N2O emission may be higher). However, due to the use of different literature sources to determine the 95% CI, we could not establish such correlations and assumed independence of factors.46,47 Where possible, however, dependency was included via calculation. NO and N2 emission from storage were calculated according to their ratio related to N2O emission and therefore are interrelated (Table 2). Similarly, NO3 leaching was calculated depending on gaseous emissions (N2O and NH3) (Section 2.3.2). Other factors affecting NO leaching, such as uncertainty in N- uptake by crops, were excluded. Overall, the dependent factors

will change according to the randomly selected values of the emission factors during Monte Carlo simulation. The assumed independency between emission factors however will most likely lead to an overestimated uncertainty in the final results.
30: 2.1. LCA Approach and Impact Assessment. We used LCA to compare the environmental impact of segregating pig urine and feces with the impact of conventional manure management, that is, liquid manure storage, transport, and field application. Environmental consequences from changing manure management were included in the system boundary (i.e., consequential LCA). 16 This implied that, for electricity supply and mineral fertilizer production, we used so-called marginal suppliers. Marginal suppliers were based on ref 2. The geographical scope of the study was Western Europe, where The Netherlands was considered for marginal suppliers, compositions of manure, urine and feces, and NO leaching.

We evaluated five environmental impact categories most relevant to manure management: climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), marine eutrophication (ME), particulate matter formation (PMF), and fossil fuel depletion (FFD). 2,9 For PMF, we included only NH3, NO and SO2 as precursors. Direct emission of PM 10 was excluded, because this was not affected by the different management systems. For 12 eutrophication, we excluded phosphorus (P) leaching as in all systems an equal amount of P entered and left the system. Field application was considered to be subject to equal management leading to unchanged leaching of P.1 To compare 2 the environmental impacts, we used the ReCiPe midpoint v.1.04
impact assessment method17 in SimaPro v.7.3 (PRé Con- sultants, The Netherlands).

2.2. Functional Unit and Manure Management Scenarios. 2.2.1. Functional Unit. The function of the considered system is to manage liquid manure or the segregated urine and feces. As the starting point is the excretion of urine and feces by the animal, we applied a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton mixed (i.e., liquid manure) or segregated fattening pig urine and feces originating from the same excreta to be able to compare scenarios. The same excreta ensured that equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter entered the system.

2.2.2. Manure Management Scenarios. The reference (ref) was based on conventional liquid manure management, including three months in-house storage under a slatted floor (60% of pen area), one month outside storage in a covered concrete tank, transport, and field application (Figure 1). The storage times represent yearly averages, as farmers are only allowed to apply manure from February 16 until the first of September in The Netherlands. 18 We assumed that manure was transported 31 km from the farm to the field by a lorry with a capacity of 32 tons. Subsequently, manure was injected into the soil of arable land with a conventional crop rotation, including potatoes, sugar beets, wheat, and onion, using a manure injector. 20 Manure application was assumed to substitute mineral N, P, and K-fertilizer by 62%, 100%, and 100%, respectively. 2,21

Scenarios with segregation of urine and feces included in- house segregation, four months outside storage of urine and feces separately (i.e., a yearly average), transport, and field application (Figure 1). Segregation was obtained by a V-shaped belt under a concrete slatted floor (see the Abstract graphic). Urine flows down the belt constantly, first to the middle of the belt and second to the end of the belt into a collection pit (i.e., by gravitational force). Feces were removed two times per day by rotating the belt. Outside storage of urine occurred in a covered concrete storage tank. Feces storage was presumed to be aerobic (as solid feces) or anaerobic (as liquid feces), depending on the use of bedding material. The use of bedding material will increase aeration in the storage of solid feces, leading to higher (de) nitrification and N2O emission. Currently, we do not know exactly at what feces composition (mainly dry matter content) the feces behave as "solid" or when feces behave as "liquid". Therefore, we assumed that solid and liquid feces have the same composition but can behave as

2.3. Data Inventory and Assumptions. 2.3.1. Product Chemical Composition. The excretion rate and chemical composition of liquid manure in the ref was based on average data from the Dutch fattening pig sector (Table 1). The excretion rates and composition of urine and feces were based on an eight month measurement cycle, consisting of two growing periods, of an experimental setup of the Kempfarm
Table 3. Contribution of Substances to the Impact Categories for the Scenarios and Each Life Cycle Stage (LCS) Expressed per FU
"Totals include remaining substances and are rounded; therefore, they do not always correspond to the sum of the columns and rows. Seg =
segregation; st = storage; app = application; and fert = fertilizer.

12 system. Because the compositions of liquid manure, urine, and feces were taken from different literature sources and equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter had to enter the systems in the ref and scenarios for comparability reasons (FU), we scaled the compositions of urine and feces to those of liquid manure in the ref. This is illustrated in the Supporting Information for the total N composition. For the ref and scenarios, we calculated a mass balance to determine the compositions of the liquid manure and urine and feces in every life cycle stage (Table 1 and Table S1, Supporting Information).

2.3.2. Process Emissions. During storage, segregation, and field application, NH3, N2O, N, NO and CH4 emit as well as odor and nitrate (NO3-) leaches (Table 2). Emission factors for in-house storage and segregation were based on Dutch national data and ref 12, respectively. Emission factors for the Kempfarm system were measured over eight months (two growing periods) in a small-scale setup at a commercial farm. Ammonia concentrations were measured continuously, whereas greenhouse gases were measured four times (during 24 h for each sample) within the experiment. Emission factors for 12 N2O from storage of solid feces were presumed the same as those for solid manure storage (i.e., aerobic) and emission factors for storage of liquid feces were presumed the same as those for liquid manure storage (i.e., anaerobic). 30 CH4 emission factors were based on refs 2, 33, and 43. Methane emissions during storage of urine and feces were scaled relative to the ratio of emission from liquid manure in the ref (a factor of 42 times lower for solid feces and 12 times lower for urine)

based on a laboratory experiment. 43 In this study, liquid manure and separated solid and liquid fractions were stored for two months at 14 °C. Methane emissions were measured 20 times during the study.

On the basis of ref 2, we assumed emissions from application of urine to be similar to those of dewatered liquid fraction (mineral concentrate), whereas emissions from application of feces were assumed similar to those of the solid fraction remaining after liquid manure separation (Table 2). We included indirect N2O emissions from NH3 and NOx emission based on ref 42.

NO3 leaches from N application to the soil and can be calculated as a leached fraction of the soil N-surplus, that is, the difference between N-input into the soil and N-uptake by crops. 40,44 N-input into the soil and N-uptake by the crops depend, among others, on the chemical composition of fertilizer products (i.e., Nmin and Norg) and related gaseous emissions during application (NH3 and N2O). We included these factors by adjusting the NO3 leaching factor of each fertilizer product for their respective composition and gaseous emissions (Tables 1 and 2). Furthermore, we used a leaching fraction of 0.43 and a 95% confidence interval of 0.36-0.51. 45 This value represents a weighted average of leaching fractions on arable land with sandy (leaching fraction of 0.56) and clay (leaching fraction of 0.34) soils in The Netherlands.
2.3.3. Background Emissions. Background emissions are emissions not directly occurring from the management system but from supporting systems, that is, electricity production,

Figure 2. Results of the uncertainty analysis for the ref and scenarios with segregation after 1000 Monte Carlo simulations. Bars represent mean values; error bars represent the 95% confidence intervals. Different letters above bars indicate significant differences.
transportation, mineral fertilizer production, and capital goods production. All environmental impact data related to back- ground systems were taken from the Ecoinvent database. 19

2.4. Uncertainty Analysis. Uncertainty analysis was done to achieve insight in how variation in emission factors, that is, their 95% confidence intervals (95% CI), affected final results and the comparison of scenarios. We used Monte Carlo simulation (1000 runs) for the separate scenarios and for comparisons of scenarios to assess the probability of higher and lower outcomes of the respective comparison. Differences were assumed significant when outcomes were higher/lower in >97.5% of the comparisons (i.e., two-sided test at P < 0.05).

The following emission factors and related impact categories were included in the uncertainty analysis: N2O, NH3, CH4, and NO (CC, TA, PMF, and ME). For assessed emission factors, we defined a probability density function (PDF) and the 95% CI. PDFs and CIs were based on literature data (Table 2). Overall, PDFs were assumed as normal distributions (i.e., Gaussian), whereas for N2O emission we applied a log-normal distribution. 46

Ideally, within Monte Carlo simulation, a correlation matrix for dependent factors is established (e.g., when NH3 emission from application is low, N2O emission may be higher). However, due to the use of different literature sources to determine the 95% CI, we could not establish such correlations and assumed independence of factors.46,47 Where possible, however, dependency was included via calculation. NO and N2 emission from storage were calculated according to their ratio related to N2O emission and therefore are interrelated (Table 2). Similarly, NO3 leaching was calculated depending on gaseous emissions (N2O and NH3) (Section 2.3.2). Other factors affecting NO leaching, such as uncertainty in N- uptake by crops, were excluded. Overall, the dependent factors

will change according to the randomly selected values of the emission factors during Monte Carlo simulation. The assumed independency between emission factors however will most likely lead to an overestimated uncertainty in the final results.
32: 2.1. LCA Approach and Impact Assessment. We used LCA to compare the environmental impact of segregating pig urine and feces with the impact of conventional manure management, that is, liquid manure storage, transport, and field application. Environmental consequences from changing manure management were included in the system boundary (i.e., consequential LCA). 16 This implied that, for electricity supply and mineral fertilizer production, we used so-called marginal suppliers. Marginal suppliers were based on ref 2. The geographical scope of the study was Western Europe, where The Netherlands was considered for marginal suppliers, compositions of manure, urine and feces, and NO leaching.

We evaluated five environmental impact categories most relevant to manure management: climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), marine eutrophication (ME), particulate matter formation (PMF), and fossil fuel depletion (FFD). 2,9 For PMF, we included only NH3, NO and SO2 as precursors. Direct emission of PM 10 was excluded, because this was not affected by the different management systems. For 12 eutrophication, we excluded phosphorus (P) leaching as in all systems an equal amount of P entered and left the system. Field application was considered to be subject to equal management leading to unchanged leaching of P.1 To compare 2 the environmental impacts, we used the ReCiPe midpoint v.1.04
impact assessment method17 in SimaPro v.7.3 (PRé Con- sultants, The Netherlands).

2.2. Functional Unit and Manure Management Scenarios. 2.2.1. Functional Unit. The function of the considered system is to manage liquid manure or the segregated urine and feces. As the starting point is the excretion of urine and feces by the animal, we applied a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton mixed (i.e., liquid manure) or segregated fattening pig urine and feces originating from the same excreta to be able to compare scenarios. The same excreta ensured that equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter entered the system.

2.2.2. Manure Management Scenarios. The reference (ref) was based on conventional liquid manure management, including three months in-house storage under a slatted floor (60% of pen area), one month outside storage in a covered concrete tank, transport, and field application (Figure 1). The storage times represent yearly averages, as farmers are only allowed to apply manure from February 16 until the first of September in The Netherlands. 18 We assumed that manure was transported 31 km from the farm to the field by a lorry with a capacity of 32 tons. Subsequently, manure was injected into the soil of arable land with a conventional crop rotation, including potatoes, sugar beets, wheat, and onion, using a manure injector. 20 Manure application was assumed to substitute mineral N, P, and K-fertilizer by 62%, 100%, and 100%, respectively. 2,21

Scenarios with segregation of urine and feces included in- house segregation, four months outside storage of urine and feces separately (i.e., a yearly average), transport, and field application (Figure 1). Segregation was obtained by a V-shaped belt under a concrete slatted floor (see the Abstract graphic). Urine flows down the belt constantly, first to the middle of the belt and second to the end of the belt into a collection pit (i.e., by gravitational force). Feces were removed two times per day by rotating the belt. Outside storage of urine occurred in a covered concrete storage tank. Feces storage was presumed to be aerobic (as solid feces) or anaerobic (as liquid feces), depending on the use of bedding material. The use of bedding material will increase aeration in the storage of solid feces, leading to higher (de) nitrification and N2O emission. Currently, we do not know exactly at what feces composition (mainly dry matter content) the feces behave as "solid" or when feces behave as "liquid". Therefore, we assumed that solid and liquid feces have the same composition but can behave as

In one scenario, it was considered that solid feces were stored aerobically in an open shed, whereas in a second scenario liquid feces were stored anaerobically in a covered concrete tank. We assumed transport distances equal to the ref. Subsequently, urine was applied on the field with a manure injector.
