Urine and feces were applied to the same arable crop rotation
as in the ref. Environmental impact related to the production and use of bedding materials was excluded.

2.3. Data Inventory and Assumptions. 2.3.1. Product Chemical Composition. The excretion rate and chemical composition of liquid manure in the ref was based on average data from the Dutch fattening pig sector (Table 1). The excretion rates and composition of urine and feces were based on an eight month measurement cycle, consisting of two growing periods, of an experimental setup of the Kempfarm
Table 3. Contribution of Substances to the Impact Categories for the Scenarios and Each Life Cycle Stage (LCS) Expressed per FU
"Totals include remaining substances and are rounded; therefore, they do not always correspond to the sum of the columns and rows. Seg =
segregation; st = storage; app = application; and fert = fertilizer.

12 system. Because the compositions of liquid manure, urine, and feces were taken from different literature sources and equal amounts of nutrients and dry matter had to enter the systems in the ref and scenarios for comparability reasons (FU), we scaled the compositions of urine and feces to those of liquid manure in the ref. This is illustrated in the Supporting Information for the total N composition. For the ref and scenarios, we calculated a mass balance to determine the compositions of the liquid manure and urine and feces in every life cycle stage (Table 1 and Table S1, Supporting Information).

2.3.2. Process Emissions. During storage, segregation, and field application, NH3, N2O, N, NO and CH4 emit as well as odor and nitrate (NO3-) leaches (Table 2). Emission factors for in-house storage and segregation were based on Dutch national data and ref 12, respectively. Emission factors for the Kempfarm system were measured over eight months (two growing periods) in a small-scale setup at a commercial farm. Ammonia concentrations were measured continuously, whereas greenhouse gases were measured four times (during 24 h for each sample) within the experiment. Emission factors for 12 N2O from storage of solid feces were presumed the same as those for solid manure storage (i.e., aerobic) and emission factors for storage of liquid feces were presumed the same as those for liquid manure storage (i.e., anaerobic). 30 CH4 emission factors were based on refs 2, 33, and 43. Methane emissions during storage of urine and feces were scaled relative to the ratio of emission from liquid manure in the ref (a factor of 42 times lower for solid feces and 12 times lower for urine)

based on a laboratory experiment. 43 In this study, liquid manure and separated solid and liquid fractions were stored for two months at 14 °C. Methane emissions were measured 20 times during the study.

On the basis of ref 2, we assumed emissions from application of urine to be similar to those of dewatered liquid fraction (mineral concentrate), whereas emissions from application of feces were assumed similar to those of the solid fraction remaining after liquid manure separation (Table 2). We included indirect N2O emissions from NH3 and NOx emission based on ref 42.

NO3 leaches from N application to the soil and can be calculated as a leached fraction of the soil N-surplus, that is, the difference between N-input into the soil and N-uptake by crops. 40,44 N-input into the soil and N-uptake by the crops depend, among others, on the chemical composition of fertilizer products (i.e., Nmin and Norg) and related gaseous emissions during application (NH3 and N2O). We included these factors by adjusting the NO3 leaching factor of each fertilizer product for their respective composition and gaseous emissions (Tables 1 and 2). Furthermore, we used a leaching fraction of 0.43 and a 95% confidence interval of 0.36-0.51. 45 This value represents a weighted average of leaching fractions on arable land with sandy (leaching fraction of 0.56) and clay (leaching fraction of 0.34) soils in The Netherlands.
2.3.3. Background Emissions. Background emissions are emissions not directly occurring from the management system but from supporting systems, that is, electricity production,

Figure 2. Results of the uncertainty analysis for the ref and scenarios with segregation after 1000 Monte Carlo simulations. Bars represent mean values; error bars represent the 95% confidence intervals. Different letters above bars indicate significant differences.
transportation, mineral fertilizer production, and capital goods production. All environmental impact data related to back- ground systems were taken from the Ecoinvent database. 19

2.4. Uncertainty Analysis. Uncertainty analysis was done to achieve insight in how variation in emission factors, that is, their 95% confidence intervals (95% CI), affected final results and the comparison of scenarios. We used Monte Carlo simulation (1000 runs) for the separate scenarios and for comparisons of scenarios to assess the probability of higher and lower outcomes of the respective comparison. Differences were assumed significant when outcomes were higher/lower in >97.5% of the comparisons (i.e., two-sided test at P < 0.05).

The following emission factors and related impact categories were included in the uncertainty analysis: N2O, NH3, CH4, and NO (CC, TA, PMF, and ME). For assessed emission factors, we defined a probability density function (PDF) and the 95% CI. PDFs and CIs were based on literature data (Table 2). Overall, PDFs were assumed as normal distributions (i.e., Gaussian), whereas for N2O emission we applied a log-normal distribution. 46

Ideally, within Monte Carlo simulation, a correlation matrix for dependent factors is established (e.g., when NH3 emission from application is low, N2O emission may be higher). However, due to the use of different literature sources to determine the 95% CI, we could not establish such correlations and assumed independence of factors.46,47 Where possible, however, dependency was included via calculation. NO and N2 emission from storage were calculated according to their ratio related to N2O emission and therefore are interrelated (Table 2). Similarly, NO3 leaching was calculated depending on gaseous emissions (N2O and NH3) (Section 2.3.2). Other factors affecting NO leaching, such as uncertainty in N- uptake by crops, were excluded. Overall, the dependent factors

will change according to the randomly selected values of the emission factors during Monte Carlo simulation. The assumed independency between emission factors however will most likely lead to an overestimated uncertainty in the final results.
34: <header>3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION </header>

3.1. Impact of Segregation with Solid Feces. Segregation with solid feces reduced CC with 66% compared to the ref (Table 3). This reduction was due to lower CH4 production from methanogenic bacteria during in-house segregation, as compared to storage of liquid manure in the ref (328 VS 23.5 kg CO2-eq). Storage of solid feces however resulted in higher N2O emission from (de) )nitrification (56.5 kg CO2-eq) than outside storage of liquid manure in the ref (0.83 kg CO2-eq). Moreover, application of urine and feces resulted in higher total N2O emission (107 kg CO2-eq) than application of liquid manure in the ref (total 10.5 kg CO2-eq). Higher N2O emissions from storage and application in the segregation scenario however did not counteract the reduction in CH4 emission. The segregation scenario with solid feces therefore resulted in a net reduction of GHGs.

Segregation with solid feces also reduced TA by 29% and PMF by 22% compared to the ref (Table 3). This was mainly due to lower NH3 emission from in-house segregation compared to conventional manure storage (2.48 VS 5.11 kg SO2-eq). Reduced in-house NH3 emission counteracted increased NOx emission (0.14 kg SO2-eq) from (de)- nitrification during the storage of feces and increased NH3 emissions during the application.

Segregation increased ME by 11% compared to the ref (Table 3), because of increased NO emission during the storage of solid feces. FFD was hardly affected (difference of 0.36 kg oil-eq), because it was mainly related to the total
replaced mineral fertilizer and to a lesser extent to the transport, which were both similar in the scenario with segregation and the ref.

3.2. Impact of Segregation with Liquid Feces. Segregation with liquid feces reduced CC by 82% compared to the ref (Table 3). This reduction was due to the same causes as in the scenario with segregation and solid feces. Storage of liquid feces however resulted in lower N2O emission compared to solid feces (3.35 VS 56.5 kg CO2-eq) due to anaerobic conditions but at the same time only slightly increased CH4 emission compared to storage of solid feces (0.05 VS 2.04 kg CO2-eq) (Tables 2 and 3).

Segregation with liquid feces reduced both TA and PMF by 49% compared to the ref. Causes were again similar to the scenario with segregation and solid feces. Additionally, liquid feces resulted in lower NO emission from (de)nitrification during storage, compared to solid feces (0.01 VS 0.14 kg SO2- eq), and lower NH3 emission during field application, as liquid feces were now injected compared to surface spreading of solid feces (0.14 VS 1.06 kg SO2-eq).

Segregation increased ME by 9% compared to the ref (Table 3). This was mainly related to higher NO3 leaching after application as a result of more N retained in the feces fraction through avoided N emissions from in-house segregation and storage. Again, FFD was hardly affected (4%) compared to the ref, as the total avoided mineral fertilizer and transport were almost equal.

3.3. Uncertainty Analysis. Figure 2 shows results of the uncertainty analysis. Mean values for CC were 318 (95% CI, 160-486) kg CO2-eq for the ref, 108 (95% CI, 48.5-180) kg CO2-eq for the scenario with segregation and solid feces, and 58.3 (95% CI, 5.32-132) kg CO2-eq for the scenario with segregation and liquid feces. Relative uncertainty was higher in the scenarios with segregation due to relatively higher N2O emission with more variation and lower CH4 emission with less variation.

Mean values for TA ranged from 2.7 (95% CI, 0.79-4.66) kg SO2-eq to 5.3 (95% CI, 4.18-6.40) kg SO2-eq and for PMF from 0.34 (95% CI, 0.074-0.58) kg PM10-eq to 0.65 (95% CI, 0.50-0.80) kg PM10-eq. The relative uncertainty was higher in the scenarios with segregation due to more variation in their NH3 emissions as compared to the ref.

Mean values for ME ranged from 0.91 (95% CI, 0.78-1.04) kg N-eq to 1.01 (95% CI, 0.86-1.16) kg N-eq. Relative uncertainty was similar in all scenarios, as it was mainly related to NO3 leaching, having similar variation in all scenarios.

Results of the ref and scenario comparisons showed that, in 99.8% and 98.6% of the runs, scenarios with segregation and liquid or solid feces, respectively, resulted in lower CC compared to the ref (Supporting Information, Figure S1) (P < 0.05). Segregation and solid feces resulted in lower values than the ref in 89.6% of the runs for TA and 82.7% of the runs for PMF (P > 0.05), whereas segregation and liquid feces resulted in lower values than the ref in 99.3% of the runs for TA and 98.6% of the runs for PMF (P < 0.05). For ME, both segregation scenarios resulted in lower values only in approximately 30% of the runs compared to the ref (P > 0.05). A comparison between segregation scenarios showed that, on average, segregation with liquid feces had a lower environmental impact compared to segregation with solid feces however these differences were not significant (P > 0.05).
3.4. General Discussion. The outcomes show that segregating fattening pig urine and feces significantly reduced

CC and additionally segregation with liquid feces significantly reduced TA and PMF compared to the ref. Mean values for ME were increased in the scenarios with segregation. Shifting of N emissions within and between impact categories and life cycle stages was highlighted by considering the entire life cycle of manure management, thus emphasizing the necessity of a life cycle perspective when addressing environmental improvement options. Nitrogen was lost in different chemical compounds (i.e., NH3, N2O, NO and NO3) depending on factors, such as manure product type and storage system. For example, segregation and solid feces reduced NH3 emissions from in- house segregation but increased NO emission from storage and NH3 emission during application, whereas segregation and liquid feces increased NO3 leaching through more N retained in the fertilizer products. These changes affected TA, PMF, and ME. Moreover, within CC, N2O and CH4 from storage varied depending on the oxygen maintained in the storage environ- ment; that is, more CH4 was produced under anaerobic conditions, whereas more N2O was produced under aerobic conditions. Differences in N2O emission for in-house segregation with liquid or solid feces could not be established due to lack of data. Both N2O and CH4 contribute in a considerably different extent to CC (a multiplier of 298 for N2O and 25 for CH4 5

Using urine and feces for crop nutrition may enhance crop N-uptake compared to liquid manure when management, for example, application timing, can be improved. In turn, this reduces NO leaching and ME. However, efficient use of N remains subject to many factors, including soil characteristics, weather conditions, and N application standards, and therefore requires further attention for quantification under different circumstances. 44

A critical management factor affecting environmental performance in scenarios with segregation was the storage condition (i.e., aerobic or anaerobic) and the related manage- ment of feces. On average, segregation with liquid feces resulted in a lower environmental impact compared to segregation with solid feces. It would therefore be preferable to avoid solid feces production. The most promising management alternative would be to anaerobically digest the solid feces reducing the need for aerobic storage; less N2O and little CH4 emit during storage of digestate. Other alternatives include composting 48,49 or drying, but these technologies have shown to increase the environmental impact.

Methane emissions in the scenarios with segregation were low compared to the ref, as they were mainly related to the enteric fermentation of pigs. 12 Furthermore, emissions of CH4 from the storage of urine and feces contributed little to the total (approximately 8%). Methane emissions from the liquid and solid fractions were reported to be lower compared to liquid manure due to less available carbon in the liquid fraction and increased oxygen contents during storage of the solid fractions, that is, leading to increased N2O emission. The used differences in CH4 emission between liquid manure and urine and feces during outside storage however were high compared to other data. In ref 50, measured differences between liquid manure, separated liquid fraction, and solid fraction were 1.3 times higher and 4.9 times lower, respectively. On the one hand, this might indicate that CH4 emissions from urine and feces storage are underestimated here. On the other hand, the emission factors and ratios in ref 50 were determined under different circumstances (e.g., 30 days at 25 °C). Hence, determining appropriate emission factors remains critical and

requires additional monitoring in practice. However, when testing the effect of lower CH4 emission ratios for urine and feces compared to liquid manure, conclusions did not change (CC increased 5%), highlighting the relatively small contribu- tion to CC.

Segregation provides a potential to reduce NH3 emission compared to conventional manure management and can contribute to achieving the goals of the NEC. When all pig manure in The Netherlands (11.8 million tons in 2010) would be segregated, assuming liquid feces, this provides a reduction of roughly 13 kiloton NH3 (11% of total NH3 emitted: 122 kiloton in The Netherlands in 2010). Furthermore, segregation reduces in-house odor emission (approximately 70%) com- pared to conventional manure storage and may result in less nuisance in residential areas, as critical threshold values will not be exceeded as often. Odor impacts however remain strongly dependent on local climatologic conditions and the location of the farm.1 24

Other issues not considered here include animal welfare and costs of the systems. The possibility of adding bedding material with the Kempfarm system provides the potential to improve animal welfare, as bedding material results for instance in less tail biting with 51,52 Environmental issues resulting from the addition of bedding material, such as PMF, require further investigation. Costs of an animal house including the Kempfarm segregation system were reported to be similar as compared to conventional housing systems, representing a potential to reduce environmental impact at similar costs. 12 Economic consequences of the manure management chain including manure offset costs however were out of scope.

In the scenarios with segregation, FFD did not change compared to conventional manure management. However, segregation offers an opportunity to reduce transportation and as such FFD and CC. Differences in transportation were not included here, as they depend on the specific location of farms and were studied in detail by others (e.g., see refs 2 and 4). Transportation of manure products is especially important in locations with high livestock densities with local nutrient surpluses (e.g., P). Therefore, liquid manure or other fractions have to be transported to other regions. In the scenarios with segregation, transport of feces with most of the P will reduce the need of transporting water, and thus weight, reducing FFD and CC. Moreover, when feces are exported and applied on soils with low P-saturation, this may reduce overall P-leaching, as the P-surplus will be reduced in soils more close to the farms. 53 However, such leaching and management changes also depend on the specific location and were excluded here.

Uncertainty analysis in LCAs is essential, but due to several reasons, such as lack of data and knowledge of distribution functions, it is very often not included.4 46 In this study, results of uncertainty analysis supported that segregation reduced CC and additionally segregation with liquid feces reduced TA and PMF compared to the ref. Uncertainty was strongly related to variation in NH3 emission and to variation in N2O emission. Similar results were found by other authors.4 46,54 On the one hand, this highlights the necessity to include appropriate emission factors and their variation in relation to the studied circumstances. On the other hand, the variation of emission factors originates from different sources, including feed, type of system and management, and climatologic conditions (i.e., representing different sources of uncertainty, e.g., see refs 14 and 55). The variation of emission factors used here included different sources, as they were taken from different literature

references and measured over different time periods and systems. Sources of variation in emission factors therefore could not fully be uncoupled and require more attention when aiming at reducing uncertainty in the end results. Nevertheless, including such analysis, with the best available data, is essential for determining the magnitude of uncertainty in the end results that affect interpretation of results and conclusions, but even more important, final decisions.

references and measured over different time periods and systems. Sources of variation in emission factors therefore could not fully be uncoupled and require more attention when aiming at reducing uncertainty in the end results. Nevertheless, including such analysis, with the best available data, is essential for determining the magnitude of uncertainty in the end results that affect interpretation of results and conclusions, but even more important, final decisions.
36: <header>3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION </header>

3.1. Impact of Segregation with Solid Feces. Segregation with solid feces reduced CC with 66% compared to the ref (Table 3). This reduction was due to lower CH4 production from methanogenic bacteria during in-house segregation, as compared to storage of liquid manure in the ref (328 VS 23.5 kg CO2-eq). Storage of solid feces however resulted in higher N2O emission from (de) )nitrification (56.5 kg CO2-eq) than outside storage of liquid manure in the ref (0.83 kg CO2-eq). Moreover, application of urine and feces resulted in higher total N2O emission (107 kg CO2-eq) than application of liquid manure in the ref (total 10.5 kg CO2-eq). Higher N2O emissions from storage and application in the segregation scenario however did not counteract the reduction in CH4 emission. The segregation scenario with solid feces therefore resulted in a net reduction of GHGs.

Segregation with solid feces also reduced TA by 29% and PMF by 22% compared to the ref (Table 3). This was mainly due to lower NH3 emission from in-house segregation compared to conventional manure storage (2.48 VS 5.11 kg SO2-eq). Reduced in-house NH3 emission counteracted increased NOx emission (0.14 kg SO2-eq) from (de)- nitrification during the storage of feces and increased NH3 emissions during the application.

Segregation increased ME by 11% compared to the ref (Table 3), because of increased NO emission during the storage of solid feces. FFD was hardly affected (difference of 0.36 kg oil-eq), because it was mainly related to the total
replaced mineral fertilizer and to a lesser extent to the transport, which were both similar in the scenario with segregation and the ref.

3.2. Impact of Segregation with Liquid Feces. Segregation with liquid feces reduced CC by 82% compared to the ref (Table 3). This reduction was due to the same causes as in the scenario with segregation and solid feces. Storage of liquid feces however resulted in lower N2O emission compared to solid feces (3.35 VS 56.5 kg CO2-eq) due to anaerobic conditions but at the same time only slightly increased CH4 emission compared to storage of solid feces (0.05 VS 2.04 kg CO2-eq) (Tables 2 and 3).

Segregation with liquid feces reduced both TA and PMF by 49% compared to the ref. Causes were again similar to the scenario with segregation and solid feces. Additionally, liquid feces resulted in lower NO emission from (de)nitrification during storage, compared to solid feces (0.01 VS 0.14 kg SO2- eq), and lower NH3 emission during field application, as liquid feces were now injected compared to surface spreading of solid feces (0.14 VS 1.06 kg SO2-eq).

Segregation increased ME by 9% compared to the ref (Table 3). This was mainly related to higher NO3 leaching after application as a result of more N retained in the feces fraction through avoided N emissions from in-house segregation and storage. Again, FFD was hardly affected (4%) compared to the ref, as the total avoided mineral fertilizer and transport were almost equal.

3.3. Uncertainty Analysis. Figure 2 shows results of the uncertainty analysis. Mean values for CC were 318 (95% CI, 160-486) kg CO2-eq for the ref, 108 (95% CI, 48.5-180) kg CO2-eq for the scenario with segregation and solid feces, and 58.3 (95% CI, 5.32-132) kg CO2-eq for the scenario with segregation and liquid feces. Relative uncertainty was higher in the scenarios with segregation due to relatively higher N2O emission with more variation and lower CH4 emission with less variation.

Mean values for TA ranged from 2.7 (95% CI, 0.79-4.66) kg SO2-eq to 5.3 (95% CI, 4.18-6.40) kg SO2-eq and for PMF from 0.34 (95% CI, 0.074-0.58) kg PM10-eq to 0.65 (95% CI, 0.50-0.80) kg PM10-eq. The relative uncertainty was higher in the scenarios with segregation due to more variation in their NH3 emissions as compared to the ref.

Mean values for ME ranged from 0.91 (95% CI, 0.78-1.04) kg N-eq to 1.01 (95% CI, 0.86-1.16) kg N-eq. Relative uncertainty was similar in all scenarios, as it was mainly related to NO3 leaching, having similar variation in all scenarios.

Results of the ref and scenario comparisons showed that, in 99.8% and 98.6% of the runs, scenarios with segregation and liquid or solid feces, respectively, resulted in lower CC compared to the ref (Supporting Information, Figure S1) (P < 0.05). Segregation and solid feces resulted in lower values than the ref in 89.6% of the runs for TA and 82.7% of the runs for PMF (P > 0.05), whereas segregation and liquid feces resulted in lower values than the ref in 99.3% of the runs for TA and 98.6% of the runs for PMF (P < 0.05). For ME, both segregation scenarios resulted in lower values only in approximately 30% of the runs compared to the ref (P > 0.05). A comparison between segregation scenarios showed that, on average, segregation with liquid feces had a lower environmental impact compared to segregation with solid feces however these differences were not significant (P > 0.05).
3.4. General Discussion. The outcomes show that segregating fattening pig urine and feces significantly reduced

CC and additionally segregation with liquid feces significantly reduced TA and PMF compared to the ref. Mean values for ME were increased in the scenarios with segregation. Shifting of N emissions within and between impact categories and life cycle stages was highlighted by considering the entire life cycle of manure management, thus emphasizing the necessity of a life cycle perspective when addressing environmental improvement options. Nitrogen was lost in different chemical compounds (i.e., NH3, N2O, NO and NO3) depending on factors, such as manure product type and storage system. For example, segregation and solid feces reduced NH3 emissions from in- house segregation but increased NO emission from storage and NH3 emission during application, whereas segregation and liquid feces increased NO3 leaching through more N retained in the fertilizer products. These changes affected TA, PMF, and ME. Moreover, within CC, N2O and CH4 from storage varied depending on the oxygen maintained in the storage environ- ment; that is, more CH4 was produced under anaerobic conditions, whereas more N2O was produced under aerobic conditions. Differences in N2O emission for in-house segregation with liquid or solid feces could not be established due to lack of data. Both N2O and CH4 contribute in a considerably different extent to CC (a multiplier of 298 for N2O and 25 for CH4 5

Using urine and feces for crop nutrition may enhance crop N-uptake compared to liquid manure when management, for example, application timing, can be improved. In turn, this reduces NO leaching and ME. However, efficient use of N remains subject to many factors, including soil characteristics, weather conditions, and N application standards, and therefore requires further attention for quantification under different circumstances. 44

A critical management factor affecting environmental performance in scenarios with segregation was the storage condition (i.e., aerobic or anaerobic) and the related manage- ment of feces. On average, segregation with liquid feces resulted in a lower environmental impact compared to segregation with solid feces. It would therefore be preferable to avoid solid feces production. The most promising management alternative would be to anaerobically digest the solid feces reducing the need for aerobic storage; less N2O and little CH4 emit during storage of digestate. Other alternatives include composting 48,49 or drying, but these technologies have shown to increase the environmental impact.

Methane emissions in the scenarios with segregation were low compared to the ref, as they were mainly related to the enteric fermentation of pigs. 12 Furthermore, emissions of CH4 from the storage of urine and feces contributed little to the total (approximately 8%). Methane emissions from the liquid and solid fractions were reported to be lower compared to liquid manure due to less available carbon in the liquid fraction and increased oxygen contents during storage of the solid fractions, that is, leading to increased N2O emission. The used differences in CH4 emission between liquid manure and urine and feces during outside storage however were high compared to other data. In ref 50, measured differences between liquid manure, separated liquid fraction, and solid fraction were 1.3 times higher and 4.9 times lower, respectively. On the one hand, this might indicate that CH4 emissions from urine and feces storage are underestimated here. On the other hand, the emission factors and ratios in ref 50 were determined under different circumstances (e.g., 30 days at 25 °C). Hence, determining appropriate emission factors remains critical and

requires additional monitoring in practice. However, when testing the effect of lower CH4 emission ratios for urine and feces compared to liquid manure, conclusions did not change (CC increased 5%), highlighting the relatively small contribu- tion to CC.

Segregation provides a potential to reduce NH3 emission compared to conventional manure management and can contribute to achieving the goals of the NEC. When all pig manure in The Netherlands (11.8 million tons in 2010) would be segregated, assuming liquid feces, this provides a reduction of roughly 13 kiloton NH3 (11% of total NH3 emitted: 122 kiloton in The Netherlands in 2010). Furthermore, segregation reduces in-house odor emission (approximately 70%) com- pared to conventional manure storage and may result in less nuisance in residential areas, as critical threshold values will not be exceeded as often. Odor impacts however remain strongly dependent on local climatologic conditions and the location of the farm.1 24

Other issues not considered here include animal welfare and costs of the systems. The possibility of adding bedding material with the Kempfarm system provides the potential to improve animal welfare, as bedding material results for instance in less tail biting with 51,52 Environmental issues resulting from the addition of bedding material, such as PMF, require further investigation. Costs of an animal house including the Kempfarm segregation system were reported to be similar as compared to conventional housing systems, representing a potential to reduce environmental impact at similar costs. 12 Economic consequences of the manure management chain including manure offset costs however were out of scope.

In the scenarios with segregation, FFD did not change compared to conventional manure management. However, segregation offers an opportunity to reduce transportation and as such FFD and CC. Differences in transportation were not included here, as they depend on the specific location of farms and were studied in detail by others (e.g., see refs 2 and 4). Transportation of manure products is especially important in locations with high livestock densities with local nutrient surpluses (e.g., P). Therefore, liquid manure or other fractions have to be transported to other regions. In the scenarios with segregation, transport of feces with most of the P will reduce the need of transporting water, and thus weight, reducing FFD and CC. Moreover, when feces are exported and applied on soils with low P-saturation, this may reduce overall P-leaching, as the P-surplus will be reduced in soils more close to the farms. 53 However, such leaching and management changes also depend on the specific location and were excluded here.

Uncertainty analysis in LCAs is essential, but due to several reasons, such as lack of data and knowledge of distribution functions, it is very often not included.4 46 In this study, results of uncertainty analysis supported that segregation reduced CC and additionally segregation with liquid feces reduced TA and PMF compared to the ref. Uncertainty was strongly related to variation in NH3 emission and to variation in N2O emission. Similar results were found by other authors.4 46,54 On the one hand, this highlights the necessity to include appropriate emission factors and their variation in relation to the studied circumstances. On the other hand, the variation of emission factors originates from different sources, including feed, type of system and management, and climatologic conditions (i.e., representing different sources of uncertainty, e.g., see refs 14 and 55). The variation of emission factors used here included different sources, as they were taken from different literature

references and measured over different time periods and systems. Sources of variation in emission factors therefore could not fully be uncoupled and require more attention when aiming at reducing uncertainty in the end results. Nevertheless, including such analysis, with the best available data, is essential for determining the magnitude of uncertainty in the end results that affect interpretation of results and conclusions, but even more important, final decisions.

Furthermore, segregation reduces in-house odor emission (approximately 70%) com- pared to conventional manure storage and may result in less nuisance in residential areas, as critical threshold values will not be exceeded as often. Odor impacts however remain strongly dependent on local climatologic conditions and the location of the farm.1 24

Other issues not considered here include animal welfare and costs of the systems. The possibility of adding bedding material with the Kempfarm system provides the potential to improve animal welfare, as bedding material results for instance in less tail biting with 51,52 Environmental issues resulting from the addition of bedding material, such as PMF, require further investigation. Costs of an animal house including the Kempfarm segregation system were reported to be similar as compared to conventional housing systems, representing a potential to reduce environmental impact at similar costs. 12 Economic consequences of the manure management chain including manure offset costs however were out of scope.

In the scenarios with segregation, FFD did not change compared to conventional manure management. However, segregation offers an opportunity to reduce transportation and as such FFD and CC. Differences in transportation were not included here, as they depend on the specific location of farms and were studied in detail by others (e.g., see refs 2 and 4). Transportation of manure products is especially important in locations with high livestock densities with local nutrient surpluses (e.g., P). Therefore, liquid manure or other fractions have to be transported to other regions. In the scenarios with segregation, transport of feces with most of the P will reduce the need of transporting water, and thus weight, reducing FFD and CC. Moreover, when feces are exported and applied on soils with low P-saturation, this may reduce overall P-leaching, as the P-surplus will be reduced in soils more close to the farms. 53 However, such leaching and management changes also depend on the specific location and were excluded here.

Uncertainty analysis in LCAs is essential, but due to several reasons, such as lack of data and knowledge of distribution functions, it is very often not included.4 46 In this study, results of uncertainty analysis supported that segregation reduced CC and additionally segregation with liquid feces reduced TA and PMF compared to the ref. Uncertainty was strongly related to variation in NH3 emission and to variation in N2O emission. Similar results were found by other authors.4 46,54 On the one hand, this highlights the necessity to include appropriate emission factors and their variation in relation to the studied circumstances. On the other hand, the variation of emission factors originates from different sources, including feed, type of system and management, and climatologic conditions (i.e., representing different sources of uncertainty, e.g., see refs 14 and 55). The variation of emission factors used here included different sources, as they were taken from different literature

references and measured over different time periods and systems. Sources of variation in emission factors therefore could not fully be uncoupled and require more attention when aiming at reducing uncertainty in the end results. Nevertheless, including such analysis, with the best available data, is essential for determining the magnitude of uncertainty in the end results that affect interpretation of results and conclusions, but even more important, final decisions.
39: <header>3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION </header>

3.1. Impact of Segregation with Solid Feces. Segregation with solid feces reduced CC with 66% compared to the ref (Table 3). This reduction was due to lower CH4 production from methanogenic bacteria during in-house segregation, as compared to storage of liquid manure in the ref (328 VS 23.5 kg CO2-eq). Storage of solid feces however resulted in higher N2O emission from (de) )nitrification (56.5 kg CO2-eq) than outside storage of liquid manure in the ref (0.83 kg CO2-eq). Moreover, application of urine and feces resulted in higher total N2O emission (107 kg CO2-eq) than application of liquid manure in the ref (total 10.5 kg CO2-eq). Higher N2O emissions from storage and application in the segregation scenario however did not counteract the reduction in CH4 emission. The segregation scenario with solid feces therefore resulted in a net reduction of GHGs.

Segregation with solid feces also reduced TA by 29% and PMF by 22% compared to the ref (Table 3). This was mainly due to lower NH3 emission from in-house segregation compared to conventional manure storage (2.48 VS 5.11 kg SO2-eq). Reduced in-house NH3 emission counteracted increased NOx emission (0.14 kg SO2-eq) from (de)- nitrification during the storage of feces and increased NH3 emissions during the application.

Segregation increased ME by 11% compared to the ref (Table 3), because of increased NO emission during the storage of solid feces. FFD was hardly affected (difference of 0.36 kg oil-eq), because it was mainly related to the total
replaced mineral fertilizer and to a lesser extent to the transport, which were both similar in the scenario with segregation and the ref.

3.2. Impact of Segregation with Liquid Feces. Segregation with liquid feces reduced CC by 82% compared to the ref (Table 3). This reduction was due to the same causes as in the scenario with segregation and solid feces. Storage of liquid feces however resulted in lower N2O emission compared to solid feces (3.35 VS 56.5 kg CO2-eq) due to anaerobic conditions but at the same time only slightly increased CH4 emission compared to storage of solid feces (0.05 VS 2.04 kg CO2-eq) (Tables 2 and 3).

Segregation with liquid feces reduced both TA and PMF by 49% compared to the ref. Causes were again similar to the scenario with segregation and solid feces. Additionally, liquid feces resulted in lower NO emission from (de)nitrification during storage, compared to solid feces (0.01 VS 0.14 kg SO2- eq), and lower NH3 emission during field application, as liquid feces were now injected compared to surface spreading of solid feces (0.14 VS 1.06 kg SO2-eq).

Segregation increased ME by 9% compared to the ref (Table 3). This was mainly related to higher NO3 leaching after application as a result of more N retained in the feces fraction through avoided N emissions from in-house segregation and storage. Again, FFD was hardly affected (4%) compared to the ref, as the total avoided mineral fertilizer and transport were almost equal.

3.3. Uncertainty Analysis. Figure 2 shows results of the uncertainty analysis. Mean values for CC were 318 (95% CI, 160-486) kg CO2-eq for the ref, 108 (95% CI, 48.5-180) kg CO2-eq for the scenario with segregation and solid feces, and 58.3 (95% CI, 5.32-132) kg CO2-eq for the scenario with segregation and liquid feces. Relative uncertainty was higher in the scenarios with segregation due to relatively higher N2O emission with more variation and lower CH4 emission with less variation.

Mean values for TA ranged from 2.7 (95% CI, 0.79-4.66) kg SO2-eq to 5.3 (95% CI, 4.18-6.40) kg SO2-eq and for PMF from 0.34 (95% CI, 0.074-0.58) kg PM10-eq to 0.65 (95% CI, 0.50-0.80) kg PM10-eq. The relative uncertainty was higher in the scenarios with segregation due to more variation in their NH3 emissions as compared to the ref.

Mean values for ME ranged from 0.91 (95% CI, 0.78-1.04) kg N-eq to 1.01 (95% CI, 0.86-1.16) kg N-eq. Relative uncertainty was similar in all scenarios, as it was mainly related to NO3 leaching, having similar variation in all scenarios.

Results of the ref and scenario comparisons showed that, in 99.8% and 98.6% of the runs, scenarios with segregation and liquid or solid feces, respectively, resulted in lower CC compared to the ref (Supporting Information, Figure S1) (P < 0.05). Segregation and solid feces resulted in lower values than the ref in 89.6% of the runs for TA and 82.7% of the runs for PMF (P > 0.05), whereas segregation and liquid feces resulted in lower values than the ref in 99.3% of the runs for TA and 98.6% of the runs for PMF (P < 0.05). For ME, both segregation scenarios resulted in lower values only in approximately 30% of the runs compared to the ref (P > 0.05). A comparison between segregation scenarios showed that, on average, segregation with liquid feces had a lower environmental impact compared to segregation with solid feces however these differences were not significant (P > 0.05).
3.4. General Discussion. The outcomes show that segregating fattening pig urine and feces significantly reduced

CC and additionally segregation with liquid feces significantly reduced TA and PMF compared to the ref. Mean values for ME were increased in the scenarios with segregation. Shifting of N emissions within and between impact categories and life cycle stages was highlighted by considering the entire life cycle of manure management, thus emphasizing the necessity of a life cycle perspective when addressing environmental improvement options. Nitrogen was lost in different chemical compounds (i.e., NH3, N2O, NO and NO3) depending on factors, such as manure product type and storage system. For example, segregation and solid feces reduced NH3 emissions from in- house segregation but increased NO emission from storage and NH3 emission during application, whereas segregation and liquid feces increased NO3 leaching through more N retained in the fertilizer products. These changes affected TA, PMF, and ME. Moreover, within CC, N2O and CH4 from storage varied depending on the oxygen maintained in the storage environ- ment; that is, more CH4 was produced under anaerobic conditions, whereas more N2O was produced under aerobic conditions. Differences in N2O emission for in-house segregation with liquid or solid feces could not be established due to lack of data. Both N2O and CH4 contribute in a considerably different extent to CC (a multiplier of 298 for N2O and 25 for CH4 5

Using urine and feces for crop nutrition may enhance crop N-uptake compared to liquid manure when management, for example, application timing, can be improved. In turn, this reduces NO leaching and ME. However, efficient use of N remains subject to many factors, including soil characteristics, weather conditions, and N application standards, and therefore requires further attention for quantification under different circumstances. 44

A critical management factor affecting environmental performance in scenarios with segregation was the storage condition (i.e., aerobic or anaerobic) and the related manage- ment of feces. On average, segregation with liquid feces resulted in a lower environmental impact compared to segregation with solid feces. It would therefore be preferable to avoid solid feces production. The most promising management alternative would be to anaerobically digest the solid feces reducing the need for aerobic storage; less N2O and little CH4 emit during storage of digestate. Other alternatives include composting 48,49 or drying, but these technologies have shown to increase the environmental impact.

Methane emissions in the scenarios with segregation were low compared to the ref, as they were mainly related to the enteric fermentation of pigs. 12 Furthermore, emissions of CH4 from the storage of urine and feces contributed little to the total (approximately 8%). Methane emissions from the liquid and solid fractions were reported to be lower compared to liquid manure due to less available carbon in the liquid fraction and increased oxygen contents during storage of the solid fractions, that is, leading to increased N2O emission. The used differences in CH4 emission between liquid manure and urine and feces during outside storage however were high compared to other data. In ref 50, measured differences between liquid manure, separated liquid fraction, and solid fraction were 1.3 times higher and 4.9 times lower, respectively. On the one hand, this might indicate that CH4 emissions from urine and feces storage are underestimated here. On the other hand, the emission factors and ratios in ref 50 were determined under different circumstances (e.g., 30 days at 25 °C). Hence, determining appropriate emission factors remains critical and

requires additional monitoring in practice. However, when testing the effect of lower CH4 emission ratios for urine and feces compared to liquid manure, conclusions did not change (CC increased 5%), highlighting the relatively small contribu- tion to CC.

Differences in N2O emission for in-house segregation with liquid or solid feces could not be established due to lack of data. Both N2O and CH4 contribute in a considerably different extent to CC (a multiplier of 298 for N2O and 25 for CH4 5

Using urine and feces for crop nutrition may enhance crop N-uptake compared to liquid manure when management, for example, application timing, can be improved. In turn, this reduces NO leaching and ME. However, efficient use of N remains subject to many factors, including soil characteristics, weather conditions, and N application standards, and therefore requires further attention for quantification under different circumstances. 44

A critical management factor affecting environmental performance in scenarios with segregation was the storage condition (i.e., aerobic or anaerobic) and the related manage- ment of feces. On average, segregation with liquid feces resulted in a lower environmental impact compared to segregation with solid feces. It would therefore be preferable to avoid solid feces production. The most promising management alternative would be to anaerobically digest the solid feces reducing the need for aerobic storage; less N2O and little CH4 emit during storage of digestate. Other alternatives include composting 48,49 or drying, but these technologies have shown to increase the environmental impact.

Methane emissions in the scenarios with segregation were low compared to the ref, as they were mainly related to the enteric fermentation of pigs. 12 Furthermore, emissions of CH4 from the storage of urine and feces contributed little to the total (approximately 8%). Methane emissions from the liquid and solid fractions were reported to be lower compared to liquid manure due to less available carbon in the liquid fraction and increased oxygen contents during storage of the solid fractions, that is, leading to increased N2O emission. The used differences in CH4 emission between liquid manure and urine and feces during outside storage however were high compared to other data. In ref 50, measured differences between liquid manure, separated liquid fraction, and solid fraction were 1.3 times higher and 4.9 times lower, respectively. On the one hand, this might indicate that CH4 emissions from urine and feces storage are underestimated here. On the other hand, the emission factors and ratios in ref 50 were determined under different circumstances (e.g., 30 days at 25 °C). Hence, determining appropriate emission factors remains critical and

requires additional monitoring in practice. However, when testing the effect of lower CH4 emission ratios for urine and feces compared to liquid manure, conclusions did not change (CC increased 5%), highlighting the relatively small contribu- tion to CC.

Segregation provides a potential to reduce NH3 emission compared to conventional manure management and can contribute to achieving the goals of the NEC. When all pig manure in The Netherlands (11.8 million tons in 2010) would be segregated, assuming liquid feces, this provides a reduction of roughly 13 kiloton NH3 (11% of total NH3 emitted: 122 kiloton in The Netherlands in 2010). Furthermore, segregation reduces in-house odor emission (approximately 70%) com- pared to conventional manure storage and may result in less nuisance in residential areas, as critical threshold values will not be exceeded as often. Odor impacts however remain strongly dependent on local climatologic conditions and the location of the farm.1 24

Other issues not considered here include animal welfare and costs of the systems. The possibility of adding bedding material with the Kempfarm system provides the potential to improve animal welfare, as bedding material results for instance in less tail biting with 51,52 Environmental issues resulting from the addition of bedding material, such as PMF, require further investigation. Costs of an animal house including the Kempfarm segregation system were reported to be similar as compared to conventional housing systems, representing a potential to reduce environmental impact at similar costs. 12 Economic consequences of the manure management chain including manure offset costs however were out of scope.

In the scenarios with segregation, FFD did not change compared to conventional manure management. However, segregation offers an opportunity to reduce transportation and as such FFD and CC. Differences in transportation were not included here, as they depend on the specific location of farms and were studied in detail by others (e.g., see refs 2 and 4). Transportation of manure products is especially important in locations with high livestock densities with local nutrient surpluses (e.g., P). Therefore, liquid manure or other fractions have to be transported to other regions. In the scenarios with segregation, transport of feces with most of the P will reduce the need of transporting water, and thus weight, reducing FFD and CC. Moreover, when feces are exported and applied on soils with low P-saturation, this may reduce overall P-leaching, as the P-surplus will be reduced in soils more close to the farms. 53 However, such leaching and management changes also depend on the specific location and were excluded here.

Uncertainty analysis in LCAs is essential, but due to several reasons, such as lack of data and knowledge of distribution functions, it is very often not included.4 46 In this study, results of uncertainty analysis supported that segregation reduced CC and additionally segregation with liquid feces reduced TA and PMF compared to the ref. Uncertainty was strongly related to variation in NH3 emission and to variation in N2O emission. Similar results were found by other authors.4 46,54 On the one hand, this highlights the necessity to include appropriate emission factors and their variation in relation to the studied circumstances. On the other hand, the variation of emission factors originates from different sources, including feed, type of system and management, and climatologic conditions (i.e., representing different sources of uncertainty, e.g., see refs 14 and 55). The variation of emission factors used here included different sources, as they were taken from different literature

references and measured over different time periods and systems. Sources of variation in emission factors therefore could not fully be uncoupled and require more attention when aiming at reducing uncertainty in the end results. Nevertheless, including such analysis, with the best available data, is essential for determining the magnitude of uncertainty in the end results that affect interpretation of results and conclusions, but even more important, final decisions.
42: <header>3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION </header>

3.1. Impact of Segregation with Solid Feces. Segregation with solid feces reduced CC with 66% compared to the ref (Table 3). This reduction was due to lower CH4 production from methanogenic bacteria during in-house segregation, as compared to storage of liquid manure in the ref (328 VS 23.5 kg CO2-eq). Storage of solid feces however resulted in higher N2O emission from (de) )nitrification (56.5 kg CO2-eq) than outside storage of liquid manure in the ref (0.83 kg CO2-eq). Moreover, application of urine and feces resulted in higher total N2O emission (107 kg CO2-eq) than application of liquid manure in the ref (total 10.5 kg CO2-eq). Higher N2O emissions from storage and application in the segregation scenario however did not counteract the reduction in CH4 emission. The segregation scenario with solid feces therefore resulted in a net reduction of GHGs.

Segregation with solid feces also reduced TA by 29% and PMF by 22% compared to the ref (Table 3). This was mainly due to lower NH3 emission from in-house segregation compared to conventional manure storage (2.48 VS 5.11 kg SO2-eq). Reduced in-house NH3 emission counteracted increased NOx emission (0.14 kg SO2-eq) from (de)- nitrification during the storage of feces and increased NH3 emissions during the application.

Segregation increased ME by 11% compared to the ref (Table 3), because of increased NO emission during the storage of solid feces. FFD was hardly affected (difference of 0.36 kg oil-eq), because it was mainly related to the total
replaced mineral fertilizer and to a lesser extent to the transport, which were both similar in the scenario with segregation and the ref.

3.2. Impact of Segregation with Liquid Feces. Segregation with liquid feces reduced CC by 82% compared to the ref (Table 3). This reduction was due to the same causes as in the scenario with segregation and solid feces. Storage of liquid feces however resulted in lower N2O emission compared to solid feces (3.35 VS 56.5 kg CO2-eq) due to anaerobic conditions but at the same time only slightly increased CH4 emission compared to storage of solid feces (0.05 VS 2.04 kg CO2-eq) (Tables 2 and 3).

Segregation with liquid feces reduced both TA and PMF by 49% compared to the ref. Causes were again similar to the scenario with segregation and solid feces. Additionally, liquid feces resulted in lower NO emission from (de)nitrification during storage, compared to solid feces (0.01 VS 0.14 kg SO2- eq), and lower NH3 emission during field application, as liquid feces were now injected compared to surface spreading of solid feces (0.14 VS 1.06 kg SO2-eq).

Segregation increased ME by 9% compared to the ref (Table 3). This was mainly related to higher NO3 leaching after application as a result of more N retained in the feces fraction through avoided N emissions from in-house segregation and storage. Again, FFD was hardly affected (4%) compared to the ref, as the total avoided mineral fertilizer and transport were almost equal.

3.3. Uncertainty Analysis. Figure 2 shows results of the uncertainty analysis. Mean values for CC were 318 (95% CI, 160-486) kg CO2-eq for the ref, 108 (95% CI, 48.5-180) kg CO2-eq for the scenario with segregation and solid feces, and 58.3 (95% CI, 5.32-132) kg CO2-eq for the scenario with segregation and liquid feces. Relative uncertainty was higher in the scenarios with segregation due to relatively higher N2O emission with more variation and lower CH4 emission with less variation.

Mean values for TA ranged from 2.7 (95% CI, 0.79-4.66) kg SO2-eq to 5.3 (95% CI, 4.18-6.40) kg SO2-eq and for PMF from 0.34 (95% CI, 0.074-0.58) kg PM10-eq to 0.65 (95% CI, 0.50-0.80) kg PM10-eq. The relative uncertainty was higher in the scenarios with segregation due to more variation in their NH3 emissions as compared to the ref.

Mean values for ME ranged from 0.91 (95% CI, 0.78-1.04) kg N-eq to 1.01 (95% CI, 0.86-1.16) kg N-eq. Relative uncertainty was similar in all scenarios, as it was mainly related to NO3 leaching, having similar variation in all scenarios.

Results of the ref and scenario comparisons showed that, in 99.8% and 98.6% of the runs, scenarios with segregation and liquid or solid feces, respectively, resulted in lower CC compared to the ref (Supporting Information, Figure S1) (P < 0.05). Segregation and solid feces resulted in lower values than the ref in 89.6% of the runs for TA and 82.7% of the runs for PMF (P > 0.05), whereas segregation and liquid feces resulted in lower values than the ref in 99.3% of the runs for TA and 98.6% of the runs for PMF (P < 0.05). For ME, both segregation scenarios resulted in lower values only in approximately 30% of the runs compared to the ref (P > 0.05). A comparison between segregation scenarios showed that, on average, segregation with liquid feces had a lower environmental impact compared to segregation with solid feces however these differences were not significant (P > 0.05).
3.4. General Discussion. The outcomes show that segregating fattening pig urine and feces significantly reduced

CC and additionally segregation with liquid feces significantly reduced TA and PMF compared to the ref. Mean values for ME were increased in the scenarios with segregation. Shifting of N emissions within and between impact categories and life cycle stages was highlighted by considering the entire life cycle of manure management, thus emphasizing the necessity of a life cycle perspective when addressing environmental improvement options. Nitrogen was lost in different chemical compounds (i.e., NH3, N2O, NO and NO3) depending on factors, such as manure product type and storage system. Differences in N2O emission for in-house segregation with liquid or solid feces could not be established due to lack of data. Both N2O and CH4 contribute in a considerably different extent to CC (a multiplier of 298 for N2O and 25 for CH4 5

Mean values for TA ranged from 2.7 (95% CI, 0.79-4.66) kg SO2-eq to 5.3 (95% CI, 4.18-6.40) kg SO2-eq and for PMF from 0.34 (95% CI, 0.074-0.58) kg PM10-eq to 0.65 (95% CI, 0.50-0.80) kg PM10-eq. The relative uncertainty was higher in the scenarios with segregation due to more variation in their NH3 emissions as compared to the ref.

Mean values for ME ranged from 0.91 (95% CI, 0.78-1.04) kg N-eq to 1.01 (95% CI, 0.86-1.16) kg N-eq. Relative uncertainty was similar in all scenarios, as it was mainly related to NO3 leaching, having similar variation in all scenarios.

Results of the ref and scenario comparisons showed that, in 99.8% and 98.6% of the runs, scenarios with segregation and liquid or solid feces, respectively, resulted in lower CC compared to the ref (Supporting Information, Figure S1) (P < 0.05). Segregation and solid feces resulted in lower values than the ref in 89.6% of the runs for TA and 82.7% of the runs for PMF (P > 0.05), whereas segregation and liquid feces resulted in lower values than the ref in 99.3% of the runs for TA and 98.6% of the runs for PMF (P < 0.05). For ME, both segregation scenarios resulted in lower values only in approximately 30% of the runs compared to the ref (P > 0.05). A comparison between segregation scenarios showed that, on average, segregation with liquid feces had a lower environmental impact compared to segregation with solid feces however these differences were not significant (P > 0.05).
3.4. General Discussion. The outcomes show that segregating fattening pig urine and feces significantly reduced

CC and additionally segregation with liquid feces significantly reduced TA and PMF compared to the ref. Mean values for ME were increased in the scenarios with segregation. Shifting of N emissions within and between impact categories and life cycle stages was highlighted by considering the entire life cycle of manure management, thus emphasizing the necessity of a life cycle perspective when addressing environmental improvement options. Nitrogen was lost in different chemical compounds (i.e., NH3, N2O, NO and NO3) depending on factors, such as manure product type and storage system. For example, segregation and solid feces reduced NH3 emissions from in- house segregation but increased NO emission from storage and NH3 emission during application, whereas segregation and liquid feces increased NO3 leaching through more N retained in the fertilizer products. These changes affected TA, PMF, and ME. Moreover, within CC, N2O and CH4 from storage varied depending on the oxygen maintained in the storage environ- ment; that is, more CH4 was produced under anaerobic conditions, whereas more N2O was produced under aerobic conditions. Differences in N2O emission for in-house segregation with liquid or solid feces could not be established due to lack of data. Both N2O and CH4 contribute in a considerably different extent to CC (a multiplier of 298 for N2O and 25 for CH4 5

Using urine and feces for crop nutrition may enhance crop N-uptake compared to liquid manure when management, for example, application timing, can be improved. In turn, this reduces NO leaching and ME. However, efficient use of N remains subject to many factors, including soil characteristics, weather conditions, and N application standards, and therefore requires further attention for quantification under different circumstances. 44

A critical management factor affecting environmental performance in scenarios with segregation was the storage condition (i.e., aerobic or anaerobic) and the related manage- ment of feces. On average, segregation with liquid feces resulted in a lower environmental impact compared to segregation with solid feces. It would therefore be preferable to avoid solid feces production. The most promising management alternative would be to anaerobically digest the solid feces reducing the need for aerobic storage; less N2O and little CH4 emit during storage of digestate. Other alternatives include composting 48,49 or drying, but these technologies have shown to increase the environmental impact.

Methane emissions in the scenarios with segregation were low compared to the ref, as they were mainly related to the enteric fermentation of pigs. 12 Furthermore, emissions of CH4 from the storage of urine and feces contributed little to the total (approximately 8%). Methane emissions from the liquid and solid fractions were reported to be lower compared to liquid manure due to less available carbon in the liquid fraction and increased oxygen contents during storage of the solid fractions, that is, leading to increased N2O emission. The used differences in CH4 emission between liquid manure and urine and feces during outside storage however were high compared to other data. In ref 50, measured differences between liquid manure, separated liquid fraction, and solid fraction were 1.3 times higher and 4.9 times lower, respectively. On the one hand, this might indicate that CH4 emissions from urine and feces storage are underestimated here. On the other hand, the emission factors and ratios in ref 50 were determined under different circumstances (e.g., 30 days at 25 °C). Hence, determining appropriate emission factors remains critical and

requires additional monitoring in practice. However, when testing the effect of lower CH4 emission ratios for urine and feces compared to liquid manure, conclusions did not change (CC increased 5%), highlighting the relatively small contribu- tion to CC.

Segregation provides a potential to reduce NH3 emission compared to conventional manure management and can contribute to achieving the goals of the NEC. When all pig manure in The Netherlands (11.8 million tons in 2010) would be segregated, assuming liquid feces, this provides a reduction of roughly 13 kiloton NH3 (11% of total NH3 emitted: 122 kiloton in The Netherlands in 2010). Furthermore, segregation reduces in-house odor emission (approximately 70%) com- pared to conventional manure storage and may result in less nuisance in residential areas, as critical threshold values will not be exceeded as often. Odor impacts however remain strongly dependent on local climatologic conditions and the location of the farm.1 24

Other issues not considered here include animal welfare and costs of the systems. The possibility of adding bedding material with the Kempfarm system provides the potential to improve animal welfare, as bedding material results for instance in less tail biting with 51,52 Environmental issues resulting from the addition of bedding material, such as PMF, require further investigation. Costs of an animal house including the Kempfarm segregation system were reported to be similar as compared to conventional housing systems, representing a potential to reduce environmental impact at similar costs. 12 Economic consequences of the manure management chain including manure offset costs however were out of scope.

In the scenarios with segregation, FFD did not change compared to conventional manure management. However, segregation offers an opportunity to reduce transportation and as such FFD and CC. Differences in transportation were not included here, as they depend on the specific location of farms and were studied in detail by others (e.g., see refs 2 and 4). Transportation of manure products is especially important in locations with high livestock densities with local nutrient surpluses (e.g., P). Therefore, liquid manure or other fractions have to be transported to other regions. In the scenarios with segregation, transport of feces with most of the P will reduce the need of transporting water, and thus weight, reducing FFD and CC. Moreover, when feces are exported and applied on soils with low P-saturation, this may reduce overall P-leaching, as the P-surplus will be reduced in soils more close to the farms. 53 However, such leaching and management changes also depend on the specific location and were excluded here.

Uncertainty analysis in LCAs is essential, but due to several reasons, such as lack of data and knowledge of distribution functions, it is very often not included.4 46 In this study, results of uncertainty analysis supported that segregation reduced CC and additionally segregation with liquid feces reduced TA and PMF compared to the ref. Uncertainty was strongly related to variation in NH3 emission and to variation in N2O emission. Similar results were found by other authors.4 46,54 On the one hand, this highlights the necessity to include appropriate emission factors and their variation in relation to the studied circumstances. On the other hand, the variation of emission factors originates from different sources, including feed, type of system and management, and climatologic conditions (i.e., representing different sources of uncertainty, e.g., see refs 14 and 55). The variation of emission factors used here included different sources, as they were taken from different literature

references and measured over different time periods and systems. Sources of variation in emission factors therefore could not fully be uncoupled and require more attention when aiming at reducing uncertainty in the end results. Nevertheless, including such analysis, with the best available data, is essential for determining the magnitude of uncertainty in the end results that affect interpretation of results and conclusions, but even more important, final decisions.
45: <header>3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION </header>

3.1. Impact of Segregation with Solid Feces. Segregation with solid feces reduced CC with 66% compared to the ref (Table 3). This reduction was due to lower CH4 production from methanogenic bacteria during in-house segregation, as compared to storage of liquid manure in the ref (328 VS 23.5 kg CO2-eq). Storage of solid feces however resulted in higher N2O emission from (de) )nitrification (56.5 kg CO2-eq) than outside storage of liquid manure in the ref (0.83 kg CO2-eq). Moreover, application of urine and feces resulted in higher total N2O emission (107 kg CO2-eq) than application of liquid manure in the ref (total 10.5 kg CO2-eq). Higher N2O emissions from storage and application in the segregation scenario however did not counteract the reduction in CH4 emission. The segregation scenario with solid feces therefore resulted in a net reduction of GHGs.

Segregation with solid feces also reduced TA by 29% and PMF by 22% compared to the ref (Table 3). This was mainly due to lower NH3 emission from in-house segregation compared to conventional manure storage (2.48 VS 5.11 kg SO2-eq). Reduced in-house NH3 emission counteracted increased NOx emission (0.14 kg SO2-eq) from (de)- nitrification during the storage of feces and increased NH3 emissions during the application.

Segregation increased ME by 11% compared to the ref (Table 3), because of increased NO emission during the storage of solid feces. FFD was hardly affected (difference of 0.36 kg oil-eq), because it was mainly related to the total
replaced mineral fertilizer and to a lesser extent to the transport, which were both similar in the scenario with segregation and the ref.

3.2. Impact of Segregation with Liquid Feces. Segregation with liquid feces reduced CC by 82% compared to the ref (Table 3). This reduction was due to the same causes as in the scenario with segregation and solid feces. Storage of liquid feces however resulted in lower N2O emission compared to solid feces (3.35 VS 56.5 kg CO2-eq) due to anaerobic conditions but at the same time only slightly increased CH4 emission compared to storage of solid feces (0.05 VS 2.04 kg CO2-eq) (Tables 2 and 3).

Segregation with liquid feces reduced both TA and PMF by 49% compared to the ref. Causes were again similar to the scenario with segregation and solid feces. Additionally, liquid feces resulted in lower NO emission from (de)nitrification during storage, compared to solid feces (0.01 VS 0.14 kg SO2- eq), and lower NH3 emission during field application, as liquid feces were now injected compared to surface spreading of solid feces (0.14 VS 1.06 kg SO2-eq).

Segregation increased ME by 9% compared to the ref (Table 3). This was mainly related to higher NO3 leaching after application as a result of more N retained in the feces fraction through avoided N emissions from in-house segregation and storage. Again, FFD was hardly affected (4%) compared to the ref, as the total avoided mineral fertilizer and transport were almost equal.

3.3. Uncertainty Analysis. Figure 2 shows results of the uncertainty analysis. Mean values for CC were 318 (95% CI, 160-486) kg CO2-eq for the ref, 108 (95% CI, 48.5-180) kg CO2-eq for the scenario with segregation and solid feces, and 58.3 (95% CI, 5.32-132) kg CO2-eq for the scenario with segregation and liquid feces. Relative uncertainty was higher in the scenarios with segregation due to relatively higher N2O emission with more variation and lower CH4 emission with less variation.

Mean values for TA ranged from 2.7 (95% CI, 0.79-4.66) kg SO2-eq to 5.3 (95% CI, 4.18-6.40) kg SO2-eq and for PMF from 0.34 (95% CI, 0.074-0.58) kg PM10-eq to 0.65 (95% CI, 0.50-0.80) kg PM10-eq. The relative uncertainty was higher in the scenarios with segregation due to more variation in their NH3 emissions as compared to the ref.

Mean values for ME ranged from 0.91 (95% CI, 0.78-1.04) kg N-eq to 1.01 (95% CI, 0.86-1.16) kg N-eq. Relative uncertainty was similar in all scenarios, as it was mainly related to NO3 leaching, having similar variation in all scenarios.
